column3: The coral-like Ni3S2 was synthesized by electrochemical anodization and cathodic deposition method in a two-electrode cell with two pieces of NF (2 cm × 3 cm) as the anodic and cathodic electrodes. NF was successively ultrasonically cleaned in acetone, ethanol, and deionized water for 20 min. A complex aqueous solution containing 1.0 M NH4F and 2.0 M thiourea was used as the electrolyte. The electrochemical anodization and cathodic deposition process were carried out at a constant potential of 4 V on a dc voltage constant-current power supply for various times of 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 h. The samples prepared for different times of 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 h are denoted as Ni3S2/NF-1, Ni3S2/NF-2, Ni3S2/NF-3, Ni3S2/NF-4, and Ni3S2/NF-5, respectively. The masses of loaded Ni3S2 are about 2.0, 2.3, 2.5, 2.8, and 3.3 mg cm–2 for Ni3S2/NF-1, Ni3S2/NF-2, Ni3S2/NF-3, Ni3S2/NF-4, and Ni3S2/NF-5, respectively.

The typical synthetic procedure for the catalysts is illustrated in Scheme 1. First, a NiFe0.10Cr0.10 alloy film was deposited on Cu NA by cathodic electrolysis of an aqueous solution containing NiCl2, Fe(NO3)3, and Cr(NO3)3 under an applied potential of −0.9 V vs RHE for 300 s. With the molar ratio of Ni2+, Fe3+, and Cr3+ adjusted to 1:0.10:0.10, an electrodeposited film denoted as NiFe0.10Cr0.10 was obtained. Then, the obtained electrode was immersed in 1 M KOH electrolyte for activation. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) scans between 1.0 and 1.8 V versus RHE were conducted to activate the surface of the NiFe0.10Cr0.10 film to an OER-active hydroxide phase, which is denoted as CS-NiFe0.10Cr0.10 (Figure S1). For comparison, CS-NiFe0.10 samples without Cr incorporation were also prepared.

Ni(OH)2, Fe-doped Ni(OH)2, and Al-doped Ni(OH)2 were deposited onto FNC or fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) electrodes through electrodeposition. Ni, NiFe, and NiAl catalysts were prepared by starting from 100 mL of a 0.1 M solution of Ni(NO3)2 or by adding 0.2 mmol of either FeCl3·6H2O or AlCl3·6H2O to the Ni(NO3)2 solution, respectively. Prior to electrodeposition, the pH of the solutions was adjusted to 6.6 and the system was purged with nitrogen gas for 1 h. Electrodeposition was performed in a three-electrode cell configuration at −16 mA/cm2 for 10 s, by using a Ag/AgCl reference electrode (RE) and a Pt mesh as the counter electrode (CE).

MnOx catalyst films were prepared by controlled-potential electrolysis of solutions containing 0.5 mM Mn2+ and 50 mM methylphosphonate (MePi) at pH 8.0. These solutions remained clear over the course of all depositions. A FTO-coated glass slide was masked to 1 cm2 and immersed in the quiescent solution. A constant potential of 0.537 V was applied to the electrode with automatic iR compensation until the total charge passed reached the desired loading of catalyst. For example, a 6 mC/cm2 loading required ∼700 s of deposition time. Following deposition, films were immersed in reagent water to remove any trace Mn2+ and MePi remaining from solution. The films were prevented from drying by quickly transferring them to a Mn2+-free solution for subsequent experiments.

The Co–Mo–P–-O was electrodeposited on Ni foam substrates using a three-electrode electrochemical system. The electrolyte was an aqueous solution containing 0.15 M CoSO4, 0.05 M Na2MoO4, 0.15 M Na3C6H5O7, and 0.5 M NaH2PO2. The Ni foam, Pt foil, and saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were used as the working electrode, the counter electrode, and the reference electrode, respectively. Prior to electrodeposition, the Ni foam of 2 cm × 2 cm was first degreased by successive 15-min ultrasonication in acetone and absolute ethanol. It then was etched in 1.0 M HCl for 15 min to remove the surface oxides. Finally, the Ni foam was rinsed by abundant distilled water and dried by N2. The electrochemical deposition was performed under a constant potential of −2 V (vs SCE) at room temperature for 5 min. The total loading amount of the as-electrodeposited Co–Mo–P–O was measured to be 3.06 mg cm–2 through weighting the weight change before and after electrodeposition. A contrast sample, called the as-electrodeposited Co-sample, was prepared using the same procedure, except the electrolyte only contains 0.15 M CoSO4 and 0.15 M Na3C6H5O7.

The FeCoNiPxSy catalysts were prepared by electrodeposition on a planar Ti foil at −1 A cm–2 without stirring. For the deposition of FeCoNiPxSy, NiSO4, FeSO4, and CoSO4 were used as the metal salts and NaH2PO2 and H2NCSNH2 were used as the salts for introducing P and S dopants. Moreover, H3BO3 and NH4Cl were added to the deposition solution as additional agents to improve the quality of the deposits. The electrolyte bath contained 0.09 M NiSO4, 0.03 M FeSO4, 0.053 M CoSO4, 0.4 M H3BO3, 0.28 M NH4Cl, and certain amounts of NaH2PO2 and H2NCSNH2. The Fe:Co:Ni metal ratio was maintained at 17:31:52 because this ratio has been reported to afford catalysts with the best electrochemical performance.  When the P and S compositions of FeCoNiPxSy catalysts were tuned, the total amounts of NaH2PO2 and H2NCSNH2 were maintained at 1 M while those of NaH2PO2 (x M) and H2NCSNH2 (y M) were systematically varied and the resulting samples are denoted FeCoNiPxSy. These samples were FeCoNiP1S0, FeCoNiP0.8S0.2, FeCoNiP0.5S0.5, FeCoNiP0.2S0.8, and FeCoNiP0S1, which were prepared with a two-electrode electrodeposition.

In order to optimize the electrodeposition time of catalysts toward the HER and OER, FeCoNiP1S0 catalysts with deposition times of 30, 60, 75, 90, 105, 120, and 150 s were prepared and evaluated. The optimized deposition time of FeCoNiP1S0 was determined to be 120 s for the HER and 105 s for the OER, respectively. As a result, all of the FeCoNiPxSy catalysts were prepared at 120 s for the HER and 105 s for the OER. In order to investigate the roles played by Fe, Co, and Ni for FeCoNiPxSy catalysts toward the HER and OER, MP0S1 (M: Fe, Co, Ni, FeCo, FeNi, and CoNi) samples with a deposition duration of 120 s for the HER and MP0.5S0.5 with a deposition duration of 105 s for the OER were prepared in their deposition solutions, respectively, in which the total content of metal salts was maintained at 0.173 M while the compositions of the metals were systematically varied.

A NF was cleaned by sonicating in 1 M HCl solution for 30 min, followed by ethanol for 15 min, cleaned subsequently with DI water, and then left to dry in air. The series of Ni1–xFex nanosheets (x: 0, 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, and 1) were prepared by electrodeposition using a three-electrode cell in which NF is used as the working electrode. Platinum wire and Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) were used as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. For electrodeposition, 6 mM Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and 6 mM Fe(NO3)3·9H2O were used, and the ratio between the precursors were varied to obtain a series of Ni1–xFex nanosheets. Deposition was carried out by applying −1.0 V for 300 s. This process led to the incorporation of bulk Fe (FeB). Once electrodeposition is completed, the material is rinsed with DI water and left to dry. After that, FeB was cycled in 1 M Fe(NO3)3 containing 1 M KOH to incorporate surface Fe (FeS).

The amorphous Ni(OH)2 nanosheets are synthesized by a simple, facile, and green electrochemistry technique (Supporting Information, S1).  Two parallel graphite electrodes are used as the cathode and anode electrodes immersed in a quartz chamber, and the distance between two electrodes is about 5 cm. A target of 99.99% pure nickel with an area of 25 × 25 mm2 is fixed at the center of a quartz chamber and immersed in highly pure deionized water (18.2 MΩ cm–1) without any chemical additives. The synthesis is carried out under a constant potential voltage of 90 V for 75 min, and the final products are deposited on the surface of the graphite sheet (electrode). The detailed formation of the amorphous Ni(OH)2 is provided in the Supporting Information (S1).

Prior to the electrodeposition, nickel foam (NF) substrate was ultrasonically washed as reported earlier . In a typical synthesis of Co-LDH, a piece of the cleaned NF (1 × 1 cm2), a Ag/AgCl electrode, and a Pt plate were served as the working electrode, the reference electrode and the counter electrode, respectively. The cathodic electrodeposition procedure was curried out at the potential of −0.94 V versus Ag/AgCl for 180 s in 45 mL Co(NO3)2 (471.4 mg) aqueous solution. Then the as-fabricated Co-LDH nanosheet arrays were treated in 10 mg/ml 2-methylimidazole aqueous solution at 30 °C for 48 h.

Ru/GDY was synthesized using an in-situ reduction strategy. Typically, a piece of as-prepared GDY was immersed in an aqueous solution containing 2 mM RuCl3 and 0.5 M H2SO4 for 2 h. After that, an electro-reduction process was carried out with a constant current density of 10 mA cm−2 for 40 s in the same solution. The as prepared Ru/GDY was wash with water for several times and used for electrochemical measurements immediately.

NiFe dendrite are prepared via a template-free electrodeposition process in two electrodes system. The metal stainless steel cylinder serves as positive electrode, meanwhile the iron wire (diameters of about 200 μm) as work electrode is vertically immersed in the center of the reactor (Fig. S1). Then the electrolyte constituted of nickel sulfate (0.72 M), ferrous sulfate (0.72 M) and l(+)-Ascorbic acid (0.057 M) is injected between the two electrodes. Metal dendrites can form on the surface of iron wire through deposition after 5min and 5 V.

In the electrochemical deposition process, hierarchical MnCo2O4 nanowire or MnCo2O4 nanosheet arrays was used as the working electrode, and the CHI660E electrochemical workstation was used with Ag/AgCl and Pt wire as reference and counter electrodes, respectively. In addition, the deposition solution (50 mL) was consisted of 5 mM cobalt dichloride hexahydrate [CoCl2·6H2O] and 0.75 M thiourea [CS(NH2)2]. The MnCo2O4 materials was performed by electrochemical deposition cyclic voltammetry (CV) technique with different cycles (tR) for electrochemical deposition (tR = 5 cycles, 7 cycles and 9 cycles) at a potential range of 1.2 to 0.2 V vs with respect to Ag/AgCl. The resulting CoS shell was then washed with deionized water and dried in vacuum at room temperature for 12 h to obtain a 3D layered core/shell nanowires or nanosheets arrays. The materials were named as MnCo2O4@CoS-tR cycles nanowires (MCO-NW@CS-tR cycles) or MnCo2O4@CoS-tR cycles nanosheets (MCO-NS@CS-tR cycles).

The porous self-supporting films were fabricated by electrodeposition. The pretreated conductive carbon papers (99.99%, 1 cm×1 cm) were used as the cathodes, and the platinum foils of 1cm×1cm were the anodes. Before electrodeposition, the conductive carbon papers were immersed in nitric acid for 12 h, and then were repeatedly rinsed with deionized water and dried in vacuum at 60 °C for 8 h. All the electrodeposition processes were conducted at a cathodic current density of 3.0 A cm−2 for 30 s at room temperature. The electrolyte composition and prepared film names were listed in Table 1. All electrodeposition solutions were prepared with fresh deionized water.

The V–Ni3S2@CoFe-LDH/NF was prepared by the method of electrodeposition in a mixed solution containing 0.01 M Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.02 M FeSO4·7H2O. The V–Ni3S2/NF sample (1 cm × 2 cm) was used as working electrode, a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode was as the reference electrode and Pt plate was as the counter electrode. The electrodeposition potential was −1.0 V vs Ag/AgCl for 50s, and the deposition process was under the protection of 5% H2/Ar gas. After the electrodeposition is over, the V–Ni3S2@CoFe-LDH/NF sample was rinsed with deionized water and dried at 60 °C in a vacuum drying oven. In contrast, CoFe-LDH/NF was synthesized by the same electrodeposition method.

The Ni0.27Co2.73O4 nanosheets were deposited onto the surface of the as-prepared TiN nanowires by a combination of an electrodeposition and a subsequent calcination. The electrodeposition was carried out in a standard three-electrode system at room temperature, using TiN nanowires on Ti foil as the working electrode, saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode and a Pt foil as the counter-electrode, respectively. Aqueous solution containing 0.195 M cobalt nitrate and 0.097 M nickel nitrate was used as electrolyte. The electrodeposition was conducted by a linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) from −0.5 to −1.1 V with a sweep rate of 10 mV/s. After electrodeposition, the sample was rinsed with distilled water and ethanol and then dried at 60 ̊C. Finally, the sample was annealed in inert gas at 250 ̊C for 1.5 h.

Electrocatalysts were fabricated on Ni foam using electrochemical deposition method under ambient conditions in a three-electrode glass cell using a ZIVE SP2 electrochemical workstation (Won-A-Tech Co. ltd, South Korea). Platinum (Pt) wire, and Ag/AgCl (1 M KCl) electrodes were used as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. Co(OH)2 and Ni(OH)2 electrocatalysts were directly deposited on Ni foam (working electrode) using an aqueous solution of 0.0125 M Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.0125 M Ni(NO3)2·6H2O at − 1.0 V (vs Ag/AgCl) for 10 min, and named as Co(OH)2@Ni and Ni(OH)2@Ni, respectively. Furthermore, Co(OH)2 and Ni(OH)2 was deposited on the top surface of Ni(OH)2@Ni and Co(OH)2@Ni samples respectively, using the same conditions as mentioned above. The respective samples were referred to as Co(OH)2/Ni(OH)2@Ni and Ni(OH)2/Co(OH)2@Ni. Similarly, NiCo LDH was deposited on Ni foam using an aqueous solution of 0.0125 M Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.0125 M Ni(NO3)2·6H2O at similar conditions and called as NiCo LDH@Ni. Prepared electrodes were washed with distilled water and dried at 60 °C.

Nickel foam (NF) was first etched with 6 M HCl for 20 min under ultrasonic followed by washing with ethanol and water to remove the oxide on the surface. The electrodeposition of NiFeP and NiFe(OH)x was carried out in a three-electrode system electrochemical cell. The cleaned nickel foam, an Ag/AgCl (3 M KCl) electrode and a graphite rod were employed as the working electrode, reference electrode, and counter electrode, respectively. To be specific, NiFeP was deposited in an aqueous solution with 3 mM NiSO4.6H2O, 3 mM FeSO4.7H2O, and 36 mM NaH2PO2·H2O by applying −1.0 V vs Ag/AgCl for 600 s; NiFe(OH)x was deposited in an aqueous solution with 3 mM Ni(NO3)2.6H2O and 3 mM Fe(NO3)3.9H2O. NiFeP and Ni(OH)2 by applying −1.0 V vs Ag/AgCl for 600 s. After electrodeposition, the NiFeP and NiFe(OH)x were rinsed with deionized water and dried in air. The nickel foam substrate can be changed to other electronic conductive substrate, e.g., FTO in this work.

The electroplating station consisted of the plating solution, a power supply and electrodes; one 70 × 50 × 1 mm 316 stainless steel sheet and one 20 × 10 × 1 mm IrO2/Ti sheet (DSA® acquired from AMAC Corrosion Co.) with one face insulated by ferro-lacquer, as counter and working electrodes, respectively. An internal half-cell vessel connecting the solutions by a Nafion membrane was used. The electrolytic solutions used in the preparation of modified EMDs are tabulated in Table 1. The samples were electrodeposited at i = 10 mA cm−2. The electrodeposited samples were dried at 100 oC in an oven for a period of 1 hour in an air atmosphere and used for all physico-chemical and electrochemical measurements.

Catalysts with specific Fe:Co ratios were prepared by electrodeposition from metal nitrate salt solutions that were degassed. After deposition, the film was rinsed briefly in Type I water and then submerged in KOH buffer. Films were held at a constant potential of 0.84 V in 1 M KOH pH 14 or 1.0 V in 0.1 M KOH pH 13 for 3 h to convert the film to the oxyhydroxide form before further electrochemical analysis. To obtain films of various thicknesses, the total deposition time was altered between 30 and 120s and the current held during deposition was changed between 0.5, 1.0 and 5.0 mA/s. The exact film loading was obtained from ICP-MS analysis of the films.

The catalysts were synthesized through a convenient and straightforward electrodeposition method by using a typical three-electrode system on a VMP-3 potentiostat (Biologic Science Instrument). A rotating disk electrode with glassy carbon (RDE, 0.196 cm−2, Pine Research Instrumentation) was used as the working electrode with a leakless Ag/AgCl (eDAQ) reference electrode and a Pt mesh counter electrode. –0.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl was applied to the RDE working electrode for the reduction of Cu and Ag ions in the electrolyte. The electrodeposition ended when a charge of 60 mC had been passed. Cu modified on RDE (Cu/RDE) and Ag modified on RDE (Ag/RDE) were electrodeposited at –0.2 V vs. Ag/AgCl from an aqueous electrolyte containing specific amounts of Cu(NO3)2 (20.0 mM, 30 mL) and AgNO3 (20.0 mM, 30 mL), respectively. For comparison, the CuxAg10−x/RDE (x = 1, 3, 5, 7, and 9) were prepared under the same conditions from precursor electrolytes (20.0 mM, 30.0 mL) containing AgNO3 and Cu(NO3)2 solutions with different concentration ratios (from 1/9 to 9/1).

To avoid the introduction of other metal composites, copper foam (0.5 cm × 0.5 cm) was used as the catalyst support and current collector. The copper foam was sequentially washed with 1.0 M HCl, ethanol, and deionized water (each for 10 min). Cu3Ag7/CF was synthesized through electrodeposition on a Gamry Interface 1000 electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode configuration, using a carbon rod and a leakless Ag/AgCl (eDAQ) electrode as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. Cu3Ag7/CF was electrodeposited at a constant current of –3.0 A cm−2 for 30 s from an aqueous electrolyte (30 mL) containing specific amounts of Ag2SO4 (14.0 mM) and CuSO4 (6.0 mM) in 1.5 M H2SO4 under Ar without stirring. In addition, 0.1 M sodium citrate was added to the precursor as a complexing agent. To avoid the interference of the background signal from copper foam in the XRD tests, we prepared Cu-modified, Ag-modified, and Cu3Ag7-modified carbon paper electrodes (Cu/CP, Ag/CP, and Cu3Ag7/CP) samples following the same preparation conditions. The only difference is that copper foam was replaced by carbon paper.

NiCeOx–Au was synthesized by electrodepositing Ni and Ce onto an Au-coated substrate which was prepared by sputter-coating 10 nm of Ti followed by 100 nm of gold onto a GC disk (Sigradur G HTW Hochtemperatur-Werkstoffe GmbH). Electrodeposition was carried out in a 100 ml beaker with a carbon rod counter electrode and a Ag/AgCl reference electrode. The beaker, carbon rod, and GC disk holder were cleaned in aqua regia, and the Ag/AgCl reference electrode was cleaned in 1 M HCl to avoid metal contamination. The deposition electrolyte (95:5 Ni:Ce by mol%) consisted of 1.105 g of nickel nitrate (99.9985%, Strem Chemicals) and 0.087 g of cerium nitrate (99.99%, Sigma-Aldrich) dissolved in 40 ml of Millipore water. The deposition current of −16 mA cm−2 was held for 10 s. Ni1−xCexOy–Au of differing Ni to Ce ratios, gold thickness and catalyst thickness, NiCeOx (95:5) on bare GC, and NiFeOx of various Ni to Fe ratios and CoCeOx (95:5 Co:Ce by mol%) on Au-coated GC and bare GC were also prepared with the same amount of charge passed for each sample.

Nickel nitrate (Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 98%, Macleans), cobalt nitrate(Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 97%, Aladdin), and ferric nitrate(Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 98.5%, Macleans) were used as the precursors to prepare the FeCoNi(OH)x nanosheets. The FeCoNi(OH)x nanosheets were electrodeposited on MoS2 nanosheets in a standard three-electrode electrochemical system, where the CF/VGSs/MoS2, Pt foil, and Ag/AgCl were used as the working, counter, and reference electrode, respectively. The aqueous solution with 0.1 M Ni(NO3)2, 0.1 M Co(NO3)2, and 0.05 M Fe(NO3)3 was used as the electrodepositing solution. The electrodeposition was carried out for 200 s at a constant cathode voltage of −1.0 V. After electrodeposition, the obtained composite electrode was washed several times with deionized water and ethanol, and dried at 70 °C in the air. For comparison, FeCoNi(OH)x nanosheets were also directly electrodeposited on the carbon felt following the above stated conditions. The mass loading of VGSs, MoS2, FeCoNi(OH)x, and FeCoNiPx were determined by weighing the samples before and after growth. The mass loading of VGSs, MoS2, and FeCoNi(OH)x in the CF/VMFO is about 1.1, 1.7, and 6.7 mg/cm2.

The corresponding metal hydroxide precursors were first synthesized on the substrates by electrodeposition from a solution of the corresponding metal nitrate(s) with a total concentration of 0.1 molar (mol). For synthesizing the Ce-doped Co(OH)2 precursor, 10 mol percent (mol%) of Co(NO3)2 in the solution was replaced with Ce(NO3)3. Note that the as-received carbon paper substrate (Fuel Cell Earth, TGP-H-060) was Teflon-coated; therefore, it was first treated with oxygen plasma at 300 W power for 15 min for each side and then annealed in air at 700 °C for 1 h to make the surface hydrophilic. Prior to the electrodeposition, the FTO and carbon paper substrates were successively washed with acetone, ethanol, and nanopure water. During the electrodeposition, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode and a Pt mesh counter electrode were used, and a constant potential of –1.0 V vs. Ag/AgCl was applied on the substrates for 3 and 10 min in the case of FTO and carbon paper, respectively. During the electrodeposition, the reduction of nitrate generated OH– and a local alkaline environment near the substrate, and subsequently metal hydroxides were formed on the substrate:

hp-Ni/NF anode was prepared by a facile template-free cathodic electrodeposition of porous Ni microsphere arrays on a pre-shaped Ni foam (0.5 × 0.5 cm2). Typically, the deposition was performed in a standard two-electrode configuration at room temperature with an electrolyte of 2.0 M NH4Cl and 0.1 M NiSO4. Pre-shaped commercial Ni foam and platinum wire were used as the working electrode and counter electrode, respectively. The electrodeposition was carried out at a constant current of −2 A cm−2 for 500 s to obtain hp-Ni/NF samples.

Three-dimensional hierarchical porous nickel (hp-Ni) catalyst was prepared by a facile template-free cathodic electrodeposition of porous Ni microspheres onto Ni foam (NF) substrate, i.e., hp-Ni/NF (see Supplementary Fig. 1). Hydrogen bubbles evolve at cathodic potential via HER during electrodeposition, responsible for the porous structure. The anode was obtained by activating the as-prepared hp-Ni/NF electrode at a constant current density of 50 mA cm−2 until a stable OER performance was reached. During activation, the hp-Ni/NF electrode was used as the working electrode, and a Pt wire acted as the counter electrode. An Ag/AgCl (sat. KCl) electrode served as the reference electrode, which was calibrated with potassium ferricyanide prior to each usage. To fabricate the cathode, the surface of hp-Ni/NF electrode was converted to Ni2P/NF in a chemical vapor deposition system (see Methods and Supplementary Fig. 2 for details).

Electrodeposition was carried out in a standard three-electrode electrochemical cell with Ni foam (NF 1 cm2) as the working electrode, a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, and Pt as the counter electrode using a CHI 760E electrochemical station. The electrolyte consisted of 8 mM NiCl2·6H2O, 42 mM CoCl2·6H2O, 50 mM SeO2 and 100 mM LiCl. All chemicals used in this work were of analytical grade and did not need any further purification. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used to electrodeposit Ni–Co–Se in the potential range of −0.9 V to 0.2 V vs. SCE with a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 at 30 °C for 3 cycles. Before electrodeposition, NF was sonicated in 3.0 M HCl solution with ethanol and deionized (DI) water for 10 min. For comparison, NiSe2 and CoSe2 were electrodeposited in the electrolyte without CoCl2·6H2O or NiCl2·6H2O, respectively.

Prior to the deposition, Cu mesh was sonicated in acetone, 3 M HCl, DI H2O, and ethanol successively, each for 5 min. The thin Ni layer was electrodeposited from an aqueous plating solution containing 1.65 M NiSO4, 0.5 M H3BO3, and 0.05 M l-histidine. The electrodeposition was conducted in a three-electrode system with a piece of clean and dry Cu mesh (1 × 1 cm2, thickness 100 μm) as the working electrode, a Pt wire as the counter electrode, and a mercury–mercurous sulfate electrode as the reference electrode. A constant potential of −2.5 V was applied to the working electrode at room temperature for 4000 s to obtain the Ni-plated Cu mesh.

The Co0 nanoparticles were then electrogenerated within the film by the reduction of the cobalt oxalate complex inserted into the polymer at an applied potential of −1.3 V vs. Ag/AgCl for a cathodic charge of 4 mC (Fig. S1†). Of note, in this article, the potentials are given versus Ag/AgCl for all electrochemical experiments performed in aqueous solution. During this electroreduction step, the C/PPN+ modified electrode is maintained in the cobalt oxalate solution (0.1 M borate buffer at pH 6). For comparison purposes, Co0 particles were also electrodeposited on naked C electrodes (without PPN+) from the same cobalt oxalate aqueous solution using also a cathodic charge of 4 mC. Typically for Co0 electrodeposition on C and in C/PPN+ electrodes, about 130–170 s are required to pass a cathodic charge of 4 mC (Fig. S1†).

For a typical synthesis of a core–shell structure, the ultrathin NiFe LDH nanosheets were grown uniformly on the as-synthesized hydrous NiMoO4 nanorod arrays through the electrodeposition method at a constant cathodic current density of −20 mA cm−2 for 250 s in a typical three-electrode system. The as-prepared NiMoO4·xH2O/NF, a Pt plate and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were adopted as the working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively. The electrolyte was obtained by dissolving Ni(NO3)2·6H2O (0.15 M) and FeSO4·7H2O (0.15 M) in 100 mL of ultrapure water under the protection of an argon atmosphere. After electrodeposition, the obtained electrode was washed with DI water and ethanol followed by drying in air. To determine the optimal electrodeposition time of 250 s, NiMoO4@NiFe LDH-y (y = 50, 150, 350 s) catalysts were synthesized by the same method. In contrast, the NiFe LDH/NF catalyst was obtained solely from NF using the same electrodeposition procedure.

The Ni–Fe–Se hollow nanospheres were electrodeposited on nickel foam by the chronoamperometric technique. The electrodeposition was carried out using a potentiostat (CHI 760E, CH Instruments) with a three-electrode configuration at room temperature. Prior to the electrodeposition, the nickel foam (thickness: 1.0 ± 0.2 mm, surface density: 300 ± 30 g m−2) was treated with 5 M HCl solution to remove the impurity and oxide layer. Next, the nickel foam was sonicated with DI water and ethanol for 15 min successively and dried at room temperature before use. A piece of cleaned nickel foam (1 cm × 2 cm), a graphite rod and a KCl-saturated Ag/AgCl electrode were used as the working, counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The electrolyte was prepared by dissolving 1.2 mmol (0.134 g) of selenium dioxide (Aladdin Reagent, AR) in 30 mL of DI water as solution A. Then, 0.8 mmol of nickel(ii) nitrate hexahydrate (Aladdin Reagent, AR) and 1.6 mmol of iron(iii) nitrate nonahydrate (Aladdin Reagent, AR) were dissolved in 10 mL of DI water as solution B. After that, solution B was added dropwise into solution A under vigorous stirring for 30 min as the electrodeposition bath. The electrodeposition bath was purged with N2 for 30 min for degassing before each electrodeposition process. The electrodeposition was performed at an applied voltage of −1.5 (vs. Ag/AgCl) with a deposition time of 10 min to obtain Ni–Fe–Se/NF. For comparison, Ni–Fe–Se/NF(−1.1) and Ni–Fe–Se/NF(−1.9) were also prepared at an applied voltage of −1.1 and −1.9 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), respectively. After electrodeposition, the obtained products were rinsed with DI water and ethanol several times and then dried at room temperature overnight. Ni–Se/NF and Fe–Se/NF were prepared at −1.5 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) by using pure nickel(ii) nitrate hexahydrate and iron(iii) nitrate nonahydrate as a metal source, respectively, without changing the total amount of the metal source (2.4 mmol) in the electrodeposition bath and other synthetic conditions.

A layer of PANI was synthesized on pre-treated CP by electrodeposition and then immersed into solutions containing different ratios of Ir3+ and W6+ to uniformly load the metal ions on PANI by electrostatic adsorption. After carbonization at 900 °C in a tube furnace under a N2 atmosphere, Ir1−xWx@NC was synthesized. In this process, the content and spatial distribution of metal ions in the polyaniline framework can be synergistically regulated by the ratio of quinonoid imine and reducing benzene ring amine, which is adjusted by a constant potential method, in the electrodeposited PANI. After the carbonization treatment, the metal/polyaniline composite is converted into a 3D network of self-supporting mixed catalysts on the N-doped carbon skeleton. On exposure to ambient air, the obtained metals located on N-doped carbon are oxidized to metal oxides rapidly. These catalysts in situ growing on the CP can be directly used as working electrodes in a catalytic reaction. In order to demonstrate the effect of the iridium and tungsten content on catalytic activity, the molar ratio of iridium to tungsten in the initial solution was varied while the sum Ir + W = 0.1 M was kept constant (Ir/W = 1/0, 9/1, 5/5, 1/9, 0/1 and blank PANI after carbonization, denoted as Ir@NC, Ir0.9W0.1@NC, Ir0.5W0.5@NC, Ir0.1W0.9@NC, W@NC and NC, respectively) to prepare different samples in the same procedure (see Experimental details in the ESI†).

For the deposition of the NiFe-LDH electrocatalyst, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and Fe(NO3)2·9H2O were dissolved in a 1 : 1 molar ratio in 50 mL deionized water for 30 min at room temperature. Growth of NiFe-LDH on the NF substrate was carried out using an electrodeposition technique with a three-electrode system in potentiostatic mode. Platinum wire and a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode were used as the counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively, and NF (0.5 cm × 0.5 cm) served as the working electrode. Deposition was performed at −1.0 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) for 5, 10, or 15 min at room temperature. After deposition, the as-deposited NF was carefully rinsed with ethanol and deionized water several times to remove untreated residues and dried overnight at 60 °C.

Typically, a piece of well-cutted Ni foam (NF) was ultrasonically washed by 6 M HCl solution to remove impurities from the surface, and then rinsed with water and ethanol in sequence, followed by drying in air. The preparation procedure of the CoOx–CoSe film is illustrated in Scheme S1.† The electrolytic deposition was performed in a custom-built single-compartment electrochemical cell configured with NF as the working electrode, a parallel positioned gauze platinum electrode as the counter electrode and a commercial saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode. The deposition bath includes 10 mM CoCl2·6H2O, 10 mM SeO2 and 50 mM KCl. The potentiostatic electrodeposition was controlled at −0.7 V versus SCE to obtain the amorphous CoOx–CoSe film on NF. After deposition, the NF working electrode was taken out from the electrolyte solution and carefully cleaned by water and ethanol, and then dried by using N2. For the CoOx–CoSe 3D electrode, the loading amount of 1.7 (for OER) and 3.5 mg cm−2 (for HER) was obtained with an electrodeposition time of 20 and 40 min, respectively.

Electrodeposition was carried out in a simple two-electrode cell by galvanostatic electrolysis, and a graphite electrode was used as the counter electrode (spectral grade, 1.8 cm2) and carbon fiber cloth (CFC) (Phychemi Company, Hong Kong) was utilized as the working electrode (∼0.5 cm × 2 cm). The CFC was first dipped in a HCl solution (3 mol L−1) for 15 min, and then washed with ethanol and water in turn. The ZnO NRAs were obtained by electrodeposition in a solution of 10 mM Zn(NO3)2 + 50 mM NH4NO3 at a current density of 0.8 mA cm−2 at 70 °C for 90 min.

GDY nanowalls on CF (1 cm×0.5 cm) were adopted as the working electrode for electrodeposition, a carbon rod as the counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl aqueous solution) electrode as the reference electrode. The electrolyte for electrodeposition contained NiCl2 (0.2 M), CoCl2 (0.2 M), NaH2PO2 (0.2 M), and NH4Cl (0.25 M) with water. The Ni−Co−P, Ni−P or Co−P was electrodeposited on the surface of GDY nanowalls at 0.01 A/cm2 for 5 min at room temperature, respectively. For comparative study, the Ni−Co−P was also directly electrodeposited on the bare CF with the similar methods.

Synthesis of NPC-CP: Commercially available carbon paper was first calcinated at 350 °C for 1 h in a muffle oven and then functionalized by oxidation treatment with concentrated HNO3 (65 wt%) under ambient conditions for 40 min to generate oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., carboxylic groups) on the surface. The polyaniline hydrogel on CP was synthesized through an electrochemical method in a three-electrode cell. The pretreated CP, Ag/AgCl, and Pt wire were employed as the working electrode, reference electrode, and counter electrode, respectively. In a typical run, 5 mL of aniline was slowly added into ethylene diphosphonic acid (EDPA) aqueous solution under vigorous stirring. After half an hour, the resultant mixed solution was used as the electrolyte. Generally, a flow of nitrogen was bubbled through the electrolyte solution for at least 30 min prior to electrochemical treatment and kept during the whole process. Cyclic voltammogram (CV) technique with a sweep rate of 50 mV s−1 was used to initialize the electrodeposition and polymerization of aniline monomers. The color of the CP changes from silvery gray through light green to deep green. After cycling 20 times, the resulting electrode was subjected to alternative water and ethanol washing, and drying at 60 °C overnight. The as-prepared polyaniline hydrogel grown on CP was transferred into a tube furnace and at 700 °C for 2 h in flowing N2 atmosphere, followed by free cooling to ambient temperature. The loading amount of the chemically doped carbon nanofibers on CP was about 0.6 mg cm−2. For comparison, N-doped carbon nanofibers, IrO2, and scratched N,P-codoped carbon nanofibers coated on carbon paper using polymeric binders were also prepared (see the Supporting Information for detailed synthesis procedures).

3DP GC electrodes were first pretreated by O2 plasma for 3 min. Then, Ni-NiO was grown on 3DP GC (T-3DP GC, Bulk GC/Ni-NiO, CC/Ni-NiO, CP/Ni-NiO) by a facile electrodeposition process in a 20 mm Ni(CH3COO)2 aqueous electrolyte. The chronoamperometry method with −3 V versus SCE for 1 h was used in this process. The obtained sample was washed with DI water three times and dried at 70 °C.

For comparison, the Ni deposited layer on Ni foam was prepared via the same procedure as described above, with the cleaned Ni foam as the scaffold instead of the ZnO nanoarray. Then, the solid Ni@NiCo2O4 material was obtained after NiCo2O4 nanosheets were directly deposited on the Ni layer by the electrodeposition procedure as described above.

The nickel foam was ultrasonicated in acetone, 10% HCl aqueous solution, and DI water successively and then vacuum-dried at 60 °C. A previous method of preparation of Fe-doped nickel sulfide (FeNi2S4)  was suitably modified to support dual borate/Fe doping and catalyst deposition on a nickel foam. Briefly, 1.5 mmol FeSO4 7H2O, 2 mmol NiSO4·6H2O, 1.5 mmol K2B4O7·4H2O, and 8 mmol l-cysteine were dissolved in 40 mL of a glycol–DI water mixture (1:1 v/v) and rigorously stirred for 30 min. This mixture and a pretreated nickel foam (3 cm × 2 cm) were transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 8 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the nickel foam was removed, washed with water several times, and dried at 60 °C in a vacuum. For comparative studies, Fe-free borate-doped Ni3S2 was also prepared by the same procedure sans the use of iron sulfate. Likewise, borate/Fe-free undoped Ni3S2 was also prepared without Fe and borate precursors. The catalyst loading on NF was in the range of 4–6 mg cm–2.

Ligand L1 (10 mg, 0.03 mmol), sodium terephthalate (6.2 mg, 0.03 mmol), and Co(NO3)2·6H2O (8.6 mg, 0.03 mmol) were taken in a glass tube. To this, 3 mL of methanol and 7 mL of deionized water were added and the glass tube was sealed and heated at 100 °C for 2 days and then slowly cooled to room temperature, resulting in red-colored single crystals of MOF 1. Yield 70–75%.

Before the synthesis of materials, a piece of nickel foam (the geometric size is 4 cm × 2 cm) was carefully treated with diluted hydrochloric acid (3 M), acetone, and deionized water in sequential order under ultrasonic conditions for 30 min each time. Next, 32 mL of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), 2 mL of deionized water, and 2 mL of ethanol were mixed in a glass container. Subsequently, 0.1246 g (0.75 mmol) of BDC was dissolved in the mixed solution under continuous stirring. Then, 0.1188 g (0.5 mmol) of NiCl2·6H2O and 0.0320 g (0.25 mmol) of VCl2 were added. A green solution formed under continuous magnetic stirring for 30 min. After that, the mother solution was ultrasonically shocked (40 kHz) with a piece of pretreated nickel foam for 8 h at room temperature. Afterward, the mother solution and nickel foam were diverted into a 50 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (PTFE) autoclave, which was then sealed and heated at 140 °C for 24 h. After being cooled down to room temperature, nickel foam was taken out and washed with ethanol (3–4 times), while the powder deposited on the bottom of the autoclave was obtained through centrifugation. Finally, the products were vacuum-dried at 60 °C for 12 h and were denoted Ni2V-MOFs@NF. Three other MOF@NF electrocatalysts (e.g., NiV-MOFs@NF, NiV2-MOFs@NF, and Ni-MOFs@NF) were prepared according to the same procedure except for the atomic ratios of NiCl2·6H2O to VCl2.

A homogeneous solution consisting of 0.197 mmol of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.125 mmol of NaMoO4·2H2O in 15 mL of H2O was poured into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. After the addition of the above Ni(OH)2-grown nickel foam, the autoclave was maintained for 6 h at 110 °C. The nickel foam was dried at 50 °C overnight after rinsing with water and ethanol. NiO@NiMoO4 nanosheets grown on nickel foam were obtained after being calcined at 350 °C for 2 h.

In this process, briefly, 4.0 mL of the above obtained suspension of Ni nanoparticles, 20 mL of toluene, and 1.0 mL of 1-dodecanethiol were mixed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave with a capacity of 30 mL and then kept inside an electric oven at 180 °C for 4 h. After the reaction, it was then cooled naturally. The product was collected by washing and centrifuging with anhydrous ethanol three times and finally air-dried at 60 °C.

The NXXX-Co3O4 were synthesized through a simple hydrothermal reaction. Briefly, 58 mg of Co(NO)3·H2O and 4 mg of NaNO3 were dissolved in 10 mL of water to form a homogeneous solution. A piece of carbon paper (CP) (1 cm × 4 cm) was immersed in the above solution, and we then added 9 mL of ammonium hydroxide. The mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and kept at 140 °C for 12 h. After being washed with deionized water and ethanol several times, the product was dried at room temperature. This NXXX-Co3O4 product was designated as N1-Co3O4. When the amounts of ammonium hydroxide were 18 and 27 mL, the Co3O4 products were denoted as N2-Co3O4 and N3-Co3O4, respectively.

CFP was cut into 10 mm × 60 mm pieces and ultrasonically cleaned by ethanol, H2SO4 solution, and water in sequence, resulting in a hydrophilic surface. Then, Co(NO3)2·6H2O (0.87 g), CO(NH2)2 (0.90 g), and NH4F (0.22 g) were dissolved in 80 mL of water, and the solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. One piece of CFP was immersed in the solution. The autoclave was then heated in an electric oven at 120 °C for 7 h, and naturally cooled to room temperature. The products (CoCH NW) were rinsed ultrasonically several times with deionized water and ethanol, and dried at 80 °C in air.

Herein, a series of LuaIrbOxOHy (Ln = lanthanides) with the ratio of a/b equal to 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.25, 2, and 3 were hydrothermally synthesized following the 300 °C calcination process. A fixed volume of 3.75 mL of 36.417 mM H2IrCl6·xH2O was added in the Teflon-lined vessel, followed by the addition of the specific volume of 0.1 M Ln(NO3)3·6H2O according to the a/b value in this work. 3 mL of 1 M NaOH was added to the solution and naturally reacted for several minutes. 17 mL of deionized water was subsequently added, and the Teflon-lined pressure vessel was encased using a stainless steel reactor and transferred to the oven. The hydrothermal synthesis condition was set to 180° for 12 h. The raw catalyst was calcined in the furnace at 300 °C, lasting for 6 h with natural cooling. The synthesis of pyrochlore Lu2Ir2O7 and IrO2 was done following the method used in our previous studies.  The low-crystallinity IrOx was synthesized at 300 °C following the above-mentioned step without the addition of lanthanides.

Ni(OH)2@IF was synthesized by the hydrothermal method. First of all, the purchased commercial iron foam (IF) is cut into pieces of 1 × 2 cm2 and washed with acetone and absolute ethyl alcohol for 20 min, respectively. The dried IF was placed in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave containing 0.281 g of Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, 0.3 g of urea, and 36 mL of deionized water. After heating for 12 h at 120 °C, IF was taken out and rinsed with deionized water several times and dried under vacuum.

Typically, 0.3 mmol (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O, 12 mmol CH4N2S, and 23 mg NGF were mixed into 70 mL distilled water and stirred for 30 min, then the mixture solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h to obtain MoS2/NGF precipitate. As for the synthesis of NiS2/NGF, Ni(OH)2/NGF was firstly obtained by using the same hydrothermal treatment to MoO2-Ni(OH)2/NGF but without the presence of Na2MoO4·2H2O. Then, NiS2/NGF was synthesized by sulfuration of Ni(OH)2/NGF, which process was similar to the sulfuration of the above MoO2-Ni(OH)2/NGF.

Synthesis of Co3S4/MnS2: 160 mg Co(NO3)2∙6H2O and 160 mg MnN2O6∙4H2O were dissolved in 20 mL ethanol and stirred for another 20 min. After the solution was completely mixed, 320 mg TAA was added to the mixture, stirred for 15 min, transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, the treated NF was added to the autoclave, and then reacted at 160 °C for 3 h.

Prior to synthesis, Ni foam (2 cm × 4 cm × 1 cm) is sonicated in 3 M HCl solution for 15 min to remove the oxide layer attached to the surface, then washed with absolute ethanol and deionized water for three times. The CoMoO4/NF precursor is prepared by a facile hydrothermal process. Firstly, 50 mL of distilled water is used to dissolve 1.45 g Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.88 g (NH4)6Mo7O24·4H2O. Meanwhile, 1.2 g CO(NH2)2 is also added as an surfactant. The total solution system is magnetic stirring for 30 min to form a homogeneous solution. Subsequently, transfer the above mixed solution to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and soak the treated NF into it. Then the autoclave is sealed at 120 °C for 10 h. After that, the NF coated with purple product, namely CoMoO4/NF precursor is collected. It is cleaned with anhydrous ethanol and distilled water alternatively and then dried at 60 °C for 12 h.

First, dissolve 0.3 g Fe(NO3)3 and 0.05 g S in the mixture of 15 ml distilled water, 5 ml ethylene glycol and 30 ml toluene and stir the mixed solution at the rate of 400 r min−1 for 2 h at room temperature. The prepared solution was then transferred to the 100 ml PTFE reactor. Next, successively put 5 mm * 5 mm Ni foam into anhydrous ethanol, acetone, 2 mol L−1 dilute hydrochloric acid and distilled water for 15 min to obtain clean Ni foam. To ensure that the Co, S-NiFe2O4 grows evenly on the front and back of the Ni foam, the treated Ni foam needs to be welded to the nickel strip, and then the nickel strip is bent so that it acts as the upholder to make the nickel foam stand upright in the 100 ml PTFE reactor liner containing the reaction solution. After assembling the reaction kettle, put it into the muffle furnace, set the temperature to 150 ℃ with a heating rate of 5 ℃ min−1, and heat it for 5 h; after cooling to room temperature, take out the reaction kettle. Co, S-NiFe2O4 was grown on nickel foam, and eventually rinse Ni foam repeatedly with ethanol and dry it.

NiMoO4 @NF precursor was in-situ grown on the surface of NF via a facile hydrothermal approach. Nickel foam (NF) (1 cm × 2 cm) was first immersed and then sonicated in 0.1 M H2SO4, acetone solution, and ethanol solution for 20 min to remove the oxide layer on the surface, then the cleaned NF were dried in a vacuum oven. Then, the same mole ratio of Ni(NO3)20.6 H2O and Na2MoO40.2 H2O were dissolved in 30 ml deionized water under stirring until completely dissolved. Afterward, the dried NF were subsequently immersed into the above solution, and then were transferred into 50 ml of Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 6 h. After cooling down, the as-obtained sample were taken out and washed with ethanol and DI water for several times, and the NiMoO4 @NF precursor were obtained after drying.

Fe foam (FF) was washed by 1 mol/L of HCl, acetone and ethanol, and then dried at 60 °C under vacuum overnight. Then 8 pieces of cleaned FFs (1 ×1.5 cm2; Fig. S1), 5 mmol of Co(NO3)2·6 H2O and 25 mmol of urea were put into 50 mL deionized (DI) water with 30 min ultrasonication to promote salt dispersion. The mixture was then transferred into 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 90 °C for 12 h to obtain CoFe-LDH/FF, which was subsequently washed by DI water, ethanol and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C.

The synthesis procedure for Mo-Ni3Fe/Ni3FeN product is illustrated in Scheme 1. The hydrothermal synthesis process of Mo doped Ni, Fe precursor was performed at 120 °C for 12 h, followed by an annealing treatment at 380 °C for 3 h in NH3 atmosphere. The as-obtained product is denoted as Mo-Ni3Fe/Ni3FeN. For comparison, the same synthesis procedure is adopted for synthesis of Ni3Fe/Ni3FeN without the addition of Mo.

In a typical synthesis, 0.3982 g of Ni(acac)2, 0.0795 g of FeCl2, 0.05 g of MoNa2O4, and 0.5608 g of C6H12N4 were dissolved in 35 mL deionized water under magnetic stirring for 30 min. The mixture was transferred into a 50 mL autoclave and maintained at 120 °C for 12 h. After hydrothermal treatment, the precursor was washed by deionized water and ethanol for three times and dried at 80 °C overnight. The as-obtained powder was calcined at 380 °C for 3 h under NH3 atmosphere with the heating rate of 5 °C min−1. The other catalysts can be also obtained with different molar ratios of Mo/Ni/Fe.

Ni3S4 hollow nanoprisms were synthesized via a solvothermal method. In total, the above Ni precursor nanoprisms (96 ​mg) was redispersed into ethanol (48 ​ml) with the addition of thioacetamide (TAA, 135 ​mg), which was averagely transferred into three sealed Teflon-lined autoclaves (20 ​mL) at 120 ​°C for 6 ​h. After cooling down to room temperature, the black precipitated product was harvested via centrifugation at 6000 ​rpm and washed 3 times with ethanol.

Ni(NO3)2.6 H2O (3 mmol) and FeCl3.6 H2O (0.2 mmol) were dissolved in an mixed solution of ultrapure water (25 mL) and methanol (15 mL) under stirring for 30 min. The obtained solution with a piece of cleaned NF were transferred into a 50 mL autoclave and maintained at 120 °C for 8 h. The as-deposited NF was washed with ultrapure water and alcohol and dried at 60 °C, and then heated at 350 °C for 2 h in air to obtain the Fe doped NiO sample (named as Ni(Fe)O). 0.2 mmol Fe doping content was selected for the subsequent synthesis due to its best activity (see Fig. S1). Then, the Ni(Fe)O (1 * 1 cm) sample was employed as working electrode to conduct cyclic voltammetry (CV) activation for 60 cycles at a rate of 50 mV s−1 (see Fig. S2). The obtained sample was further treated by OER operation and directly named as Ni(Fe)OOH.

Typically, Ni(NO3)2 and Mn(COOH)2 in a 3:1 molar ratio was dissolved into 20 mL of deionized water (metal-ion concentration 40 mM) with 560 mg of HMTA by continuously stirring. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-lined steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 80 °C for 12 h. Once cooled to room temperature, the obtained precipitate was collected and washed several times with deionized water and subsequently dried at 60 °C to yield the hierarchical NiMn LDHs. Then, the resulting NiMn LDHs were placed in a tube furnace and annealed in air at 600 °C for 4 h at a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. After natural cooling to room temperature, the porous Ni6MnO8 hierarchical microspheres were collected.

The Ni(OH)2 was prepared through an alcohothermal method. Typically, 0.4 g of NiCl2 was added into a 40 -mL ethanol solution. The mixture was stirred until a transparent solution was observed. The solution was then moved into a 50 -mL autoclave. The autoclave was then placed into an oven and treated in flowing air and heated at 150 °C for 12 h. Green powder of Ni(OH)2 was treated by 24 h ultrasound in an ethanol solution and then was collected by centrifuging the reaction mixture at a speed of 9000 r.p.m. for 10 min. The product was washed by water and ethanol four times and then was dried at 80 °C overnight. The obtained pure Ni(OH)2 was used as the reference sample and named as Ni(OH)2-r.

The w-Ni(OH)2-e was prepared through an alcohothermal method. At first, 1 g of WCl6 was added into 100 mL of ethanol to form a transparent solution. Then a certain amount of WCl6–ethanol solution was added into the NiCl2–ethanol solution (0.4g–40mL) by a pipette. After stirring for 15 min, the mixture was moved into a 50 -mL autoclave, which was then treated at 150 °C for 12 h in an oven. The product was collected by centrifuging the reaction mixture at a speed of 9000 r.p.m. for 10 min. After that, the powder was washed by water and ethanol four times and then dried at 80 °C overnight. The obtained W-doped Ni(OH)2 sample was named as w-Ni(OH)2-e.

In a typical synthesis, 121.2 mg Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, 436.5 mg Co(NO3)2·6H2O, 450 mg (NH2)2CO, and 222 mg NH4F were dissolved into 40 ml ultrapure water and moved into a 45 ml teflon-lined autoclave with steel shell. A piece of Ni foam (1 × 3 cm, thickness: 1.5 mm, bulk density: 0.3 g/cm3) was cleaned ultrasonically first with ethanol (20 ml), then with 3 M HCl aqueous solution (20 ml) for 15 min, and washed subsequently with deionized water for three times. The as-prepared Ni foam above was immersed into the above autoclave and exactly stuck at the center of the autoclave vertically. The autoclave was sealed and then heated at 120 °C for 10 h. The obtained electrode was cleaned with ethanol three times and dried in vacuum at room temperature for further characterizations and next step reaction. The synthetic methods of NiCo-LDH-NWAs and NiFe-LDH-NSAs were the same as that of FeCoNi-LDH-NWAs, except that no adding iron source for NiCo-LDH-NWAs and no adding cobalt source as well as increasing amount of iron source to 606 mg for NiFe-LDH-NSAs, respectively. When focusing on the details of reaction process, fluoride ions were added into the reaction solvent to coordinate with the metal ions, resulting in slow release of the metal ions and promotion of nucleus formation on the substrate. Then the released metal ions combined with CO3 2− and OH− generated from the hydrolysis of urea forming polymetal carbonate hydroxides.

First, 15 mg of (NH4)2MoS4 were dissolved in 38 ml of N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and stirred for 10 min to make solution homogeneous. Then 2 ml HZH (volume fraction, 85%) was introduced dropwise into the foregoing solution with vigorously stirring. The mixed solution for growing hybrid nanotubes was transferred into a 45 ml teflon-lined autoclave with steel shell and FeCoNi-LDH-NWAs was submerged into the above solution and exactly stuck at the direction of autoclave diameter vertically. The autoclave was sealed and then heated at 200 °C for 10 h. When the system was cooled down, this obtained electrode was cleaned with ethanol three times and dried in vacuum at room temperature for further characterizations. The synthetic methods of CoNi-HNTAs and NiFe-HNSAs were the same as that of FeCoNi-HNTAs, except that the precursor of FeCoNi-LDH-NWAs was replaced by NiCo-LDH-NWAs and NiFe-LDH-NSAs, respectively.

In a typical experiment, RuCl3·xH2O (13.0 mg) and Na2IrCl6·6H2O (40.5 mg) were added to a water/methanol mixed dispersion of ZIF-8 nanocubes (70–100 nm in edge length, Supplementary Fig. 1). After solvothermal treatment (80 °C for 2 h), the RuIrZnOx-U (U stands for unannealed) were obtained (Fig. 1b, Supplementary Fig. 2). RuIrZnOx-U were further annealed at 350 °C for 2 h under 0.5% O2/N2 atmosphere to obtain the RuIrZnOx hollow nanoboxes (h-nanoboxes). Similarly, RuZnOx h-nanoboxes and the samples with different Ru/Ir ratios were obtained by regulating the introduction amount of Ir4+ (Fig. 1c, Supplementary Figs. 3–5).

Generally, metal-substituted MHOF nanosheets were prepared by the reaction between NiCl2·6H2O, H2L4 and respective heterometal salts under solvothermal conditions at 120 °C. Taking Fe/Ni2(OH)2(L4) as an example, NiCl2·6H2O (90 mg, 0.38 mmol), FeCl2·4H2O (10 mg, 0.05 mmol), H2L4 (100 mg, 0.37 mmol), DMF (16 ml), water (1 ml) and ethanol (1 ml) were charged into a Pyrex vial. The mixture was heated at 120 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was collected by centrifugation, dispersed in ethanol, sonicated for 30 min and dried at 100 °C under vacuum (70 mg, 78 at.% yield). The Fe/(Ni + Fe) ratio was determined to be 12.5 at.% by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectroscopy.

A piece of nickel foam (NF) (2 cm × 1 cm × 1.6 mm) or stainless steel mesh, was immersed into a vial containing 1 ml of DI-water and 8 mg of Ni(Ac)2·4H2O and 2 mg of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O. Next, 10 mg of organic ligand 2,6-naphthalenedicarboxylate tetrahydrate was added into the above solution, and the vial was sealed for reaction at 60 °C for 20 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the nickel foam was taken out, bath ultrasonicated for 1 min and then rinsed with copious DI-water.

The Ni-doped iron oxide precursors were prepared according to a modified method reported previously. Typically, 68 mmol of NaCl was added to a mixed solution of 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol and 20 mL of deionized water. Then NiCl2·6H2O and anhydrous FeCl3 (total amount of 10 mmol) were added to the previous solution under magnetic stirring, and the amount of the Ni salt was controlled at 3%, 5%, 7%, 10%, 12%, and 15%. After complete dissolution was achieved, 25 mmol of HMTA was added and kept at constant magnetic stirring for 30 min. Then the solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and maintained at 110 °C for 12 h. Finally, the Ni-doped iron oxide precursor was obtained by centrifugation, washed several times with deionized water, and then dried at 60 °C for 10 h.

2D Fe-doped NiO nanosheets with grain boundary defects were obtained via a solvothermal reaction in PEG-200 followed by a thermally driven conversion process (Scheme 1). In the synthesis process, PEG 200 is an effective shape control agent for the formation of nanoparticles because its two –OH groups can be selectively adsorbed on certain faces to increase the growth rate of these faces and can have a steric effect to afford well-assembled secondary and tertiary branches on longer trunks. Particularly, the composition of Fe-doped NiO can be tuned by simply adjusting the ratio of the reactants. The Ni–Fe based precursors were first grown into nanosheets with good uniformity by a solvothermal method and then used as sacrificial templates for Fe-doped NiO nanosheets. Subsequently, an annealing treatment in air at 350 °C was performed for 2 h at a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 to convert the NiFe-based precursor into the 2D Fe-doped NiO nanosheets with grain boundary defects.

A two-step fabrication strategy for Ni(PO3)2–MoO3/NF is schematically illustrated in Scheme 1. The Ni(PO3)2–MoO3/NF was synthesized through a facile hydrothermal process, followed by an in situ phosphorization approach. Specifically, bare NF was immersed in an (NH4)2Mo2O7 and Ni(NO3)2 aqueous solution and heated to 150 °C. After the hydrothermal process, the NF turned yellow. Then the synthesized NiMoO4/NF with sodium hypophosphite (NaH2PO2) on the upstream side was calcined at 400 °C for 2 h under an argon atmosphere, resulting in the product Ni(PO3)2–MoO3/NF. The color of NF turned from yellow to black, implying a successful phosphorization reaction.

V–Ni3N/NF was synthesized through the following process. Typically, 1.9 mmol Ni(NO3)2, 0.1 mmol VCl3, 10 mmol urea and 5 mmol NH4F were dissolved in 40 mL water with magnetic stirring to form a homogeneous solution. Then, the solution and a piece of NF were transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After a hydrothermal reaction at 120 °C for 6 h, V-doped Ni(OH)2 was uniformly grown on the surface of NF (V–Ni(OH)2/NF). Then, the V–Ni(OH)2/NF precursor was placed in the center of a quartz tube and calcined at 400 °C for 2 h to synthesize V–NiO/NF under an N2 flow. Finally, the V–Ni3N/NF product was fabricated after annealing the V–NiO/NF in an NH3 atmosphere at 400 °C for 2 h. For comparison, the V-doped Ni3N/NF samples with different ratios of Ni and V were prepared by a similar process except for adding different initial ratios (14 : 1 and 9 : 1) of Ni(NO3)2 and VCl3 and named V–Ni3N/NF-1 and V–Ni3N/NF-2. The Ni3N/NF was obtained using the same method without adding VCl3.

N-rGOs were prepared according to the literature . 10 mg of graphene oxide were sonicated in 35 ml of water to obtain a deep brown solution followed by the addition of 10 mg of urea. Then the solution was transferred into a 50 ml autoclave and heated at 180°C for 12 h. The system was then allowed to cool to room temperature and the products were deposited at the bottom of the vessel. The final products can be well-dispersed in 20 ml of N -methylpyrrolidone (NMP) by sonication.

For the preparation of iron sulfide electrocatalysts, four sets of solvents with a fixed volume of 80 mL, were prepared by varying the ratio of EG and DI water. The ratios between DI water : EG were 0 : 80, 20 : 60, 40 : 40 and 60 : 20. About 30 mmol FeCl3 and 60 mmolthioureawere dissolved in the mixed solvent using magnetic stirring. The solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and locked for 6 hours inside an oven preheated at 190 °C. The autoclave was kept inside the oven to cool down to room temperature. Thereafter, the materials were filtered through vacuum filtration and washed several times with DI water and absolute ethanol. Then the materials were collected and kept overnight at 60 °C inside a vacuum oven. The prepared materials were denoted as FS X (like FS 20, FS 40 etc.) where X stands for the amount of EG used in the mixed solvent. FS 0 was also prepared following the previously described procedure except for the use of EG solvent was avoided.

All reagents were used without additional treatment. Ni foams were cut into rectangular pieces of 2 cm×4 cm. The Ni foam pieces were then put into 20 mL acetone to be sonicated for 20 min, and washed by deionized water for three times. The Ni foam pieces were further put into 20 mL 2 M hydrochloric acid to be sonicated for 10 min and washed by deionized water and ethanol for three times in sequence to remove nickel oxides and grease, and then dried in a 60 °C vacuum oven for 12 h. About 60 mg thioacetamide (TAA), 45 mg anhydrous sodium molybdate and 110 mg non-ionic surfactants (P123/F127/L31) were dissolved in 30 mL deionized water. The as-prepared rectangular Ni foam was then put into the above mixed solution and stirred magnetically at room temperature for 30 min. The prepared solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated to 200 °C for 24 h, where the Ni3S2/MoS2 was in-situ grown on Ni foams. The integrated foam was then washed by 20 mL deionized water and 20 mL ethanol for three times, and then dried in a 60 °C vacuum oven for 12 h.

Synthesis of IrO2/birnessite catalysts. In a typical procedure, Mn(NO3)2 (113 mmol L−1), Co(NO3)2 (170 mmol L−1) and H2IrCl6 (56.7 mmol L−1), with required volume, were dissolved in 15 mL of 1 M NaOH. After homogenization and shaking, the mixture was aged for 10 min. No assistant agent including surfactant or H2O2 was added, making the synthesis a greener and safer approach. After being tightly sealed, the autoclave was kept in an oven. The solution was slowly heated to 220 °C and was maintained at that temperature for 12 h to obtain fine crystals. The solution was then naturally cooled at ambient temperature. The brown-black precipitate was collected and transferred to a suction filter. After washing the solid several times with deionized water, the residual ions and unwanted species were almost completely removed from hydrothermally prepared crystals. The retentate on the filter was stowed in a 50 mL beaker and dried in an oven at 60 °C for dehydration. Then, the final sample was collected in a ceramic crucible and annealed at 400 °C for 6 h to achieve fine crystallinity of the as-synthesized composites. In the preparation of birnessite MnO2 (or Mn−Co(8 : 2)-Bir), a mixture of 0.5 mL of 30 % H2O2 and 15 mL of 1 M NaOH in the Mn(NO3)2 (or Mn(NO3)2 and Co(NO3)2 with required volume) precursor solution, and a dark brown suspension was immediately observed. The suspension was transferred to the autoclaves, which was later followed by the same procedure as IrO2/birnessite catalysts.

Nickel foam (NF) was cut into small pieces with dimensions of 2.0 × 3.0 cm2. These small pieces were ultrasonicated for 10 min with an aqueous solution (3.0 m HCl), ethanol, and ultrapure water, respectively. Taking Co0.4NiS@NF as an example, a mixed solution of CoCl2·6H2O (0.4 mmol) and thiourea (1.5 mmol) dissolved in ultrapure water (30 mL) was prepared and transferred into a stainless steel autoclave with Teflon liner (50 mL). Then a piece of small NF was immersed vertically into the autoclave reactor. Subsequently, the autoclave was heated in a preheated oven at 140 °C for 12 h. After reaction, the autoclave reactor was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The prepared Co0.4NiS@NF was taken out and rinsed thoroughly with ultrapure water. Thereafter, it was dried inside a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h. The preparations of Ni3S2@NF, Co0.1NiS@NF, Co0.2NiS@NF, and Co0.6NiS@NF were similar to Co0.4NiS@NF except for varying the amounts of Co precursor (CoCl2·6H2O) from 0 to 0.1, 0.2, and 0.6 mmol.

Conformal Coating of Oxidic Support (ATO Microparticles) with Ir Nanoparticles by Solvothermal Reduction in tBuOH: The solvothermal treatment of synthesized IrOx colloid was performed in glass-lined stainless steel autoclaves (Parr Series 4760 FH, PARR INSTRUMENT COMPANY). For the synthesis of unsupported Ir catalyst nanoparticles, per autoclave reaction 37.5–300 µmol previously prepared IrOx colloid were added to 90 mL of prewarmed (60 °C) tert-butanol (GRÜSSING GMBH, 99% purity). An additional amount of water and methanol at a volume ratio of 2:1 were added to obtain a final volume of 180 mL.

Co(CO3)0.5(OH)⋅0.11 H2O nanoneedle arrays were grown on carbon cloth fibers by a hydrothermal method. In a typical procedure, 70 mL solution of 0.05 m Co(NO3)2, 0.125 m NH4F, and 0.25 m urea was transferred in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave. Three pieces of carbon cloth (3 cm×3 cm) were pretreated by O2 plasma for 40 min and then dipped into the solution. Polyimide tape was employed to fix carbon cloths vertically. The autoclave was then sealed and heated at 120 °C for 6 h in furnace, followed by naturally cooling down to room temperature. Carbon cloths with pink Co(CO3)0.5(OH)⋅0.11 H2O coating were then washed with water, sonicated for 5 min, and dried at 70 °C for 3 h. As-synthesized Co(CO3)0.5(OH)⋅0.11 H2O nanoneedle arrays were used as precursors for the synthesis of CoP and Co3O4 nanoneedle arrays. Pink precipitates were also collected from the bottom of autoclave and used as precursors for the synthesis of CoP-based nanoneedle powders. CoP-based nanoneedle arrays and powders were synthesized by phosphidation of hydrothermally obtained Co(CO3)0.5(OH)⋅0.11 H2O precursor with PH3. In a typical procedure, three pieces of carbon cloth with Co(CO3)0.5(OH)⋅0.11 H2O precursor (3 cm×3 cm each) and 2.7 g of NaH2PO2⋅H2O were put in two porcelain boats.

The above synthesized ZIF-8@RF powders and nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O) were respectively dispersed in 15 mL of methanol, and then these two solutions were mixed and transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave for heating at 120 °C for 2 h. After the solvothermal reaction, the products were washed respectively with DI water and ethanol for three times, followed by centrifugation and vacuum drying at 60 °C for overnight. Similarly, the Ni(OH)2 nanocages derived from ZIF-8 were synthesized following the same procedures without resorcinol-formaldehyde layers.

Fabrication of NC–NiCu–NiCuN Nanowires Array: Nickel foam was put into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. 1 mmol NiCl2·6H2O, 2 mmol CuCl2·6H2O, and 0.15 m glucose/urea were dissolved into 15 mL deionized water. After hydrothermal reaction at 120 °C for 6 h, NiCu precursor was obtained thorough washing with deionized water and then dried at 60 °C. The NiCu precursor was annealed up to 400 °C in N2 atmosphere and then was treated in H2/NH3 atmosphere, resulting into the formation of NiCu–NiCuN nanowire array encapsulated in NC framework (noted as NC–NiCu–NiCuN). The structural characterization and electrochemical measurements of different samples have been shown in the Supporting Information.

Exfoliated black phosphorus nanosheets were synthesized by a simple liquid exfoliation method using the solvent NMP. In brief, 40 mg of bulk black phosphorus was ground in a N2-filled glovebox and transferred to an NMP solution (200 mL). The mixture was put in continuous water-bath sonication at 18 kHz for 12 h and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 15 min. The obtained supernatant was centrifuged at 10 000 rpm for 15 min and washed with N2-saturated ethanol and deionized water to get EBP. The EBP can be well redispersed in N2-saturated deionized water and sealed.

MOFs Cu@1 and Cu@3 were synthesized from the crystals of MOF 1 and 3, respectively. The crushed crystals of parent MOFs 1 (20 mg) and 3 (20 mg) were separately dipped in 20 mL of a 0.1 M ethanolic solution of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O. They were kept aside at room temperature for 2 days. Over time, the color of the materials gradually changed from bright red to deep blue. For MOF Cu@1, the complete color change was observed in 40 h, and for MOF Cu@3, the complete color change was observed in 48 h.

Au-CuOx was synthesized by using galvanic replacement reactions as schematically illustrated in Figure 1a. CuOx was prepared by annealing a Cu foam substrate at 300 °C in air. After calcination, the smooth surface of Cu foam became rugged and irregular particles were observed (Figure S1a,b). Subsequently, the CuOx substrate was immersed into 80 μL of 50 mM NaAuCl4 solution, and a galvanic replacement reaction occurred between CuOx and AuCl4– (hereafter named as Au-CuOx-80, see the SI for preparation details) by utilization of their different standard reduction potentials (, E0(Cu+/Cu) = 0.52 V).

The synthesis of FeCoP nanorods was carried out via a “one-pot” colloidal approach in the presence of octadecylamine (ODE) as a non-coordinating solvent and oleylamine (OLAM) as both a capping agent and a solvent. After degassing of the reactant mixture at 110 °C (Scheme 1a), the reaction temperature was increased to 270 °C under a nitrogen atmosphere (Scheme 1b). During this course of reaction, a change in color from a clear solution to reddish-brown ensured initiation of nucleation. Further increasing the temperature to 350 °C resulted in a black-colored precipitation, indicating the growth of the nucleus to finally obtain crystalline FCP350 nanorods (Scheme 1d). However, when the synthesis was carried out at 320 °C keeping all the other parameters unaltered (see the SI), partly crystalline urchin-like (agglomerated nanorods) FCP320 was obtained (Scheme 1c). Separately, monometallic cobalt phosphide (CP350) and iron phosphide (FP350) nanorods were also prepared using the same reaction parameters for further electrocatalytic comparisons.

A 0.2 g sample of as-synthesized Ni–Fe LDH intercalated with AQS was dispersed into 200 mL of water/ethanol (1:1, v/v) binary solution containing 1 M sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, C12H25OSO3Na) and 2 mM HCl. The suspension was shaken for 1 week to complete the exchange into the DS– form. For exfoliation, 0.02 g of Ni–Fe LDH intercalated with DS– was dispersed into 50 mL of formamide and shaken for 48 h.

Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized using a modified Hummers method.  Typically, 1 g of natural graphite (∼100 mesh) was first heated at 500 °C for 1 h. The metal ions were removed by treating with HCl. The purified graphite was stirred in a mixed solution of H2SO4 (50 mL) and KNO3 (1.2 g), and then 6 g of KMnO4 was slowly added into the suspension. After 6 h, 30 mL of Milli-Q water was added, and the suspension was kept below 80 °C in a cooling bath. After another addition of 200 mL of Milli-Q water and a slow addition of 6 mL of H2O2 (30 wt %), the suspension was stirred for another hour and finally diluted into 1000 mL using water. The suspension was repeatedly decanted for several times until the pH reached 5. Then ultrasonic treatment was used to exfoliate the GO slurry in water. A brown colloidal suspension of GO nanosheets was generally obtained after ∼2 h of ultrasonication.

For the synthesis of the Ni@NBCs catalyst, 1 g of bagasse powder, 5 mL of HCl, 30 mL of deionized water, 2 g of urea, 0.2 g of CTAB, and 0.250 g of NiCl2·6H2O were added in a 100 mL beaker and stirred at 600 rpm at room temperature for 12 h. Following that, the mixture was dried in an oven at 80 °C for 12 h. The resulting brown material was directly pyrolyzed in a tube furnace by slowly raising the temperature at a heating rate of 5 °C min–1 up to 250 °C and stabilized for 2 h under a continuous flow of argon (Ar). In the next step, the material was annealed at different temperatures: 600, 700, and 800 °C. The temperature was raised at a heating rate of 5 °C/min and stabilized at prepense temperature for 2 h under a continuous flow of Ar (10 mL/min). Then, the materials were allowed to cool at room temperature. The black materials (Ni@NBCs) were powdered and washed with deionized water to remove the unreacted precursor until the pH was neutral. The obtained materials were dried in an oven at 80 °C for 6 h, and the respective samples were labeled as Ni@NBC-600, Ni@NBC-700, and Ni@NBC-800.

NiMoO4 nanorods with tailored surface structures and compositions were first prepared by quenching hot NiMoO4 NRs in metal salt solutions at 0 °C, as depicted in Figure 1a. The nanorods were initially at 500 °C and then immersed in aqueous solutions of iron nitrate of different concentrations (0.1, 0.5 and 1 M) for rapid cooling (quenching). The products obtained by quenching are denoted herein as NiMoO4 NRs-Fe-0.1, NiMoO4 NRs-Fe-0.5, and NiMoO4 NRs-Fe-1 respectively. The product obtained by naturally cooling NiMoO4 from 500 °C to room temperature in a muffle furnace (i.e., without quenching) is denoted herein as NiMoO4 NRs-NC (NC is denoted natural cooling). A 2 wt % Fe-doped NiMoO4 NRs reference sample was also synthesized (referred to here as NiMoO4 NRs-Fe doped). Additionally, NiMoO4 NRs were also quenched in ice-cold water, 1 M Co(NO3)2, 0.5 M MnSO4, and 0.1 M Cr(NO3)3 aqueous solutions, with the obtained products denoted herein as NiMoO4 NRs-ice water, NiMoO4 NRs-Co-1, NiMoO4 NRs-Mn-0.5, and NiMoO4 NRs-Cr-0.1, respectively.

Three-dimensional Ni nanofiber electrode was fabricated through a magnetic-assisted chemical reduction procedure as reported in previous research studies.  First, 20 mL of deionized water containing 30 mM NiCl2 and 37.5 mM Na3C6H5O7 was prepared. Then, 2.5 mL N2H4 (80 wt %) was added into the same amount of deionized water. The pH of both solutions was adjusted to 13 using NaOH solution. Before mixing, argon was used to remove dissolved oxygen in the solutions for 15 min. Then, 0.5 mL of H2PdCl6 (20 mM), which acts as a rapid nucleating agent, was added into the mixed solution to obtain a gray liquid. The mixture was maintained at 333 K in a water bath under a parallel magnetic field (provided by two parallel neodymium iron boron magnets). The magnetic field here plays a role of a soft template, and the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images in Figure S1 indicate the assumption. The Ni nanofibers were formed in about 30 min after the solution was placed in the water bath. The Ni nanofibers were washed repeatedly with deionized water and ethanol. After undergoing a drying process, the cotton-like Ni nanofibers were pressed using a roller machine to obtain the 3D Ni nanofiber electrode.

The LaNiO3 samples were synthesized by the spray-freeze freeze-drying method.  The precursor solutions of lanthanum acetate and nickel lactate were prepared by dissolving the corresponding metal salts (ratio 1:1) as well as the respective gelatin amount (from 30 to 750 mg) in 100 mL of Milli-Q-quality deionized water. The total metal concentration was kept at 10 mM in all syntheses. The solutions were heated to 80 °C to allow the dissolution of the gelatin, stirred for 30 min, and left to cool off before the spray-freezing step. The ice-based precursor was prepared by spraying the starting solution into ca. 2 L of liquid nitrogen. The frozen solvent was removed in the freeze-drying step at reduced pressure (1 Pa) by employing a FreeZone Triad Freeze-Dry System 7400030 (Labconco). The following temperature protocol was employed in the procedure: −30 °C during the evacuation of the cooling chamber, followed by a gradual temperature increase (−30 °C (2 h), −25 °C (5 h), −20 °C (4 h), −15 °C (6 h), +30 °C (4 h)). The dry, foamlike precursor was carefully removed from the freeze-dryer and annealed at 700 °C for 2 h in a tube furnace in air.

The cleaned NF was immersed in 50 mL GO suspension, then treated ultrasonically for 5 min and finally dried at 60 °C. This process could be repeated several times to increase the load of GO. The obtained materials were denoted as NF@GO-n, where n was the repeated times.

0.314 g Na2WO4·2H2O, 3.0 g Na2SO4, 5.0 g sodium succinate, 5.0 g NiSO4·6H2O, and 1.44 g DMAB were dissolved into 200 mL of pure water to make the electroless plating (EP) bath. The activated carbonized wood was immersed into the above EP bath for 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 h. The prepared electrode was washed with ethanol and pure water to get the Ni-W-B/wood electrode. For comparison, Ni-W-B/NF was also prepared using the same procedure.

GO was prepared through a modified Hummers' method. Firstly, natural flake graphite, H2SO4, Na2CO3, and KMnO4 were mixed and stirred for 2 h at 0 °C; Secondly, the reactants were put at 35 °C for 30 min, 230 ml distilled water was added and heated to 100 °C for 15 min; Then an appropriate H2O2 was added and continued the reaction for 1 h; Finally, the precipitate was washed many times with dilute hydrochloric acid and distilled water, and dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 24 h.

pc-NiFe-LDH/NF was prepared by a green method concluding two simple dip-coating steps at room temperature (RT). Typically, the pretreated NF was dipped into 0.4 M Fe(NO3)3 solution for 3 s with the area of 1 × 1 cm2, then taken out rapidly, dried in the air for 10 min. Next, the above NF was dipped into 2.5 M NaOH solution for 3 s, similarly, taken out quickly and dried in the air for 5 min. Finally, the as-fabricated catalysts on NF were washed by DI water and absolute ethanol several times, and denoted as pc-NiFe-LDH/NF. The mass of active materials (pc-NiFe-LDH) on NF was calculated by subtracting the weight before two dip-coating steps from the weight after this process, and the average loading densities were about 0.5 mg cm−2.

Graphene oxide (GO) were synthesized using modified Hummer’s method . 10 g of highly pure graphite powder were dissolved in 380 ml of H2SO4 at 5 °C by stirring for 10 mins. 50 g of KMnO4 were added in the solution and stirred for 12 h at 35 °C. To make the dispersion highly diluted, 500 ml of water was added at 5 °C while stirring for 1 h and another 2 L of water was added. Next, H2O2 was added dropwise to the solution until the color of dispersion changed from dark brown to golden yellow. Then, obtained GO was filtered and washed multiple times with acetone in order to remove impurities. The final product can be obtained by drying in a vacuum oven at 40℃ overnight.

The fabrication process of pc-NiFe-LDH nanosheets supported on Ni foam (NF, 1 × 2 cm2) was illustrated in Fig. 1. At first, a piece of clean Ni foam was dipped into 0.4 M Fe(NO3)3 for approximately half of its area, after dried for 10 min in the air, the Fe(NO3)3 concentrated solution coated on the NF surface. Then, second dip-coating step was performed in 2.5 M NaOH, after dried in the air for 5 min, the brown pc-NiFe-LDH formed on the NF.

0.5 g of Cobalt phosphate powder is mixed with 2.8 g of DMF and the solution was sonicated for 30 min followed by 0.175 g of PAN was added. Aforementioned solution was kept in a magnetic stirrer for 24 h. Finally the viscous solution is taken in to 2 ml syringe. The optimized ES conditions were fixed as a flow rate of 0.3 ml/h, applying voltage is 18 kV, and collector type is flat type collector. The formed fibers were exclusively deposited over the Aluminum foil which was used as a conducting substrate. Under these optimized conditions the cobalt phosphate nanoporous fibrous material is prepared over the aluminum foil as a conducting substrate and the synthesized fibrous materials are subjected to 60 °C in furnace for drying. Finally, the synthesized material was denoted as hydrated cobalt phosphate fibrous network. The detailed synthetic procedure of cobalt phosphate fibrous network is illustrated in Scheme 1.

IOMO was prepared by means of a facile electrospinning method in the presence of ammonium molybdate (para) tetrahydrate, and polyvinylpyrrolidone (1.2 g, PVP, average M.W. 130,000), followed by high-temperature pyrolysis. Specifically, 1 mmol ammonium molybdate (para) tetrahydrate and 1.2 g of PVP were added to a mixed solvent of dimethylformamide (5 mL DMF) and alcohol (5 mL EtOH) with stirring at 80-degree for 12 h. The mixed solution was added into a 10 mL plastic syringe with a 25-gauge stainless-steel needle. The applied direct current voltage and distance between the needle and the aluminum-foil-wrapped collector were fixed at 15 kV and 12 cm, respectively.
