The precursor powders were further annealed at 650 °C for 6 h in the air to produce LaCoO3 NPs. The LaCo1-xFexO3 (x = 0.25 and 0.75) were also prepared by using Fe(NO3)3 · 9H2O to substitute Co(NO3)2 · 6H2O with an equal molar amount.

The Ba2BixSc0.2Co1.8−xO6−δ (x=0.1, 0.2, 0.3, denoted as BBSC0.1, BBSC0.2, and BBSC0.3, respectively) catalysts were synthesized by a standard combined EDTA-CA complexing sol-gel method (EDTA=ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid, CA=citric acid). Stoichiometric amounts of Ba(NO3)3, Bi(NO3)3⋅5 H2O, Sc2O3, and Co(NO3)2⋅6 H2O (all analytical grade, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd) were mixed in deionized water at 90 °C. The required amount of HNO3 was added to dissolve the Bi(NO3)3⋅5 H2O. EDTA and CA, serving as complexing agents, were added at a molar ratio of 1:1:2 for the total metal ions to EDTA and CA. For complete complexation, NH3 aqueous solution was added while heating at 90 °C under continuous stirring, until a transparent gel was obtained. The gel was heated at 250 °C for 5 h in an oven to form the precursor. Finally, the precursor was calcined at 1050 °C for 5 h in air. The same synthesis method was applied to BSCF and PBC; however, the calcination temperature was 1000 °C. The cooled powder was ground by hand using an agate mortar and pestle. Commercial IrO2 was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation, Shanghai, China.

The single/double perovskite (SP/DP) system with the nominal composition Sr2Fe0.2Co0.8Mo0.65Ni0.35O6−δ, double perovskite Sr2Fe0.8Co0.2Mo0.65Ni0.35O6−δ, and single perovskite SrFe0.5Co0.5O3−δ were synthesized by a sol-gel method. Taking Sr2Fe0.2Co0.8Mo0.65Ni0.35O6−δ (10 mmol) as an example, Sr(NO3)2 (4.2326 g), Fe(NO3)3⋅9 H2O (3.2320 g), Co(NO3)2⋅6 H2O (1.7462 g), (NH4)Mo7O24⋅4 H2O (1.1476 g), and Ni(NO3)2⋅6 H2O (1.4540 g) (all analytical grade from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) were dissolved in deionized water with the assistance of citric acid (CA, 16.8 g) and C10H16N2O8 (EDTA, 11.6 g). An aqueous NH3 solution was added to adjust the pH to ≈6. A transparent gel was obtained under continued magnetic stirring at ≈90 °C on a hotplate. Next, the gel was heated to 200 °C for 5 h to form a precursor powder. The precursor was calcined in air at 1100 °C for 10 h. SrFe0.5Co0.5O3−δ was calcined at 900 °C for 5 h in air.

Amorphous Ni79.2–xCoxNb12.5Y8.3 (x = 0 and 5 at %) alloys were synthesized using a two-stage high energy ball milling process (Figure 1). First, mechanical alloying was performed at cryogenic temperatures using a Retsch Cryomill to produce amorphous alloys (Figure 1a). Elemental powders of nickel (99.9 wt %, −100 mesh), cobalt (99.8 wt %, −100 mesh), niobium (99.99 wt %, −325 mesh), and yttrium (99.9 wt %, −40 mesh) were used as the starting material (Table S1). Powders were packed under an argon atmosphere (99.99% purity, <10 ppm O2) in stainless steel vials with two 7 mm diameter stainless steel balls. The ball to powder ratio (BPR) was set at a 10:1 ratio. Milling was performed at a frequency of 30 Hz for 6 h, while liquid nitrogen was passed around the vials to reduce the temperature to −196 °C. The particle size of crystalline Ni, Ni95Co5, and the two amorphous alloys Ni79.2Nb12.5Y8.3 and Ni74.2Co5Nb12.5Y8.3 was subsequently reduced by performing SA-HEBM (Figure 1b,c). The powder was packed in stainless steel vials under an argon atmosphere with dodecylphosphonic acid (95%, DDPA) as the surfactant and anhydrous ethanol as the solvent. SA-HEBM was performed at room temperature for 10 h using a cycle of 30 min on and 30 min off at a frequency of 30 Hz with a fixed BPR of 50:1.

The polycrystalline CaCu3Ir4O12 was synthesized using high-temperature and high-pressure (HPHT) methods. Highly pure CaCO3, CuO, and Ir powder were used as starting materials. These reagents were thoroughly mixed at a 1:3:4 mole ratio in an agate mortar and sintered using the conventional solid-state reaction at 1273 K for 24 h in air. Then, the obtained precursor was sealed into a gold capsule with 3.0 mm in diameter and length. The capsule was treated at 9 GPa and 1523 K for 30 min on a cubic anvil-type high-pressure apparatus. The sample was quenched to room temperature (RT) once the heat treatment was finished, and then, the pressure was gradually released. Perovskite-type CaIrO3 was synthesized using a similar HPHT process. A powder mixture of CaO and IrO2 with a molar ratio of 1:1 was used as the starting material. CaO was obtained by firing CaCO3 at 1273 K for 10 h in air. The HPHT treatment was performed under 2 GPa and 1773 K for 30 min.  Double perovskite La2CuIrO6 was synthesized using a conventional solid-state method under ambient conditions. The mixture of high-purity La2O3, CuO, and Ir powder was calcined in air at 1173 K for 3 days.

Briefly, Co, Fe, and Ni powders were mixed in a mole ratio of 1:3:5, and spherical Mg powder (75 μm, 10 wt % total mass) was used as the pore-making agent. The preparation process of the nanoporous FeCoNiMg alloy is illustrated in Figure 1. First, a planetary ball mill (QM-3SP4, Nanjing University Instrument Plant) was employed to mix Fe, Co, Ni, and Mg powders at 250 rpm for 6 h. Then, the blended powders were cold-pressed to obtain green compacts in a mould (Φ 12 mm × 6 mm) under a uniaxial pressure of 580 MPa for 1 min. Afterward, the green compacts were placed in a tube furnace (OTF-1200X, Hefei Kejing Materials Technology Corp.) in an alumina crucible. Before sintering, highly pure argon (>99.999%) was added into the furnace tube for 20 min at a rate of 50 mL min–1 to eliminate the air. Finally, the FeCoNiMg alloy sample was sintered at 900 °C for 30 min and naturally cooled to room temperature in an Ar atmosphere.

CFO nanopowders used in the experiment were commercially available (99.5%, Aladdin). To engineer the surface defect structure of CFO nanoparticles, we applied a previously reported Li reduction strategy.  Li powders (99.9%, Cellithium) and dimethyl carbonate (99%, Aladdin) were added in a mortar and then finely ground with CFO nanopowders for 1 h in an argon-filled glovebox. The mixture was then washed three times with deionized water. To tune the defects content at the surface of CFO nanoparticles, 4 weight ratios of Li to CFO, 2, 4, 6, and 8 wt %, were used in the experiments; the corresponding Li-reduced samples were named as CFO-2Li, CFO-4Li, CFO-6Li, and CFO-8Li, respectively.

The precursor Mo(ImH)1–xClx powder was annealed under Ar gas with ramping temperatures (5 °C/min) to a preoptimized temperature (900 °C), kept there for 4 h, and then cooled naturally to obtain N:Mo2C@NC NPs. For B- and N-codoped molybdenum carbide (B,N:Mo2C@BCN-X) NPs, different molar ratios of boric acid to Mo(ImH)1–xClx precursor (X = 1:4, 2:4, 3:4, 4:4) were ground and annealed under the above conditions. For comparison, undoped Mo2C@C NPs were also prepared by using simply annealing under an H2 atmosphere.

All the weighing and blending processes were performed in an argon atmosphere glovebox to prevent any oxidation of sodium. In brief, fresh lead and sodium were mixed and hermetically put into a casting steel mold with the dimension of 8 mm × 8 mm × 4 mm. Then, with the protection of argon atmosphere, the Pb/Na-containing steel mold was kept at 450 °C for 1 h with a ramping rate of 5 °C min–1 in a tubular furnace (OTF-1200X-L, Hefei Ke Jin Materials Technology Co., Ltd., China). After a natural cooling process to the room temperature, the obtained Pb–Na alloy was polished into 2 mm in thickness, dealloying treated in 500 mL of ultrapure water at room temperature (25 °C) for 12 h, and then dried in a vacuum dryer after being extensively washed with ethanol.

The developed materials (Co/C) were synthesized from a thermal process, as shown in Scheme 1. As a preliminary step, the mixture of solid precursors (sucrose and Co sources) was first melted at 120 °C under physical stirring to obtain a homogeneous fluid and then immediately transferred into a preheated oven (180 °C). A brown foamlike solid (Co-foam) was obtained after thermal treatment at 180 °C for 10 min (Figure S1). Subsequently, the brown solid (Co-foam) was treated at a high temperature (pyrolysis) to obtain Co/C-x, where x is the pyrolysis temperature.

Li2Ir1–xNixO3−δ (x = 0.25,0.75) oxides were synthesized by a conventional solid-state method on the basis of previous literature.  Ir black powder, NiO, and Li2CO3 (with 10% excess) were stoichiometrically mixed in a molar ratio by continuous grinding for 20 min. The mixture was then pressed into a pellet and sintered at 1080 °C for 12 h in air. The furnace was first cooled to 900 °C in 15 h and then to room temperature naturally. The pellet was finally ground into powder and stored in a dry box for further usage.

La0.6Sr0.4Co0.8Fe0.2O3−δ and Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3−δ were prepared using a modified molten salt method. For La0.6Sr0.4Co0.8Fe0.2O3−δ (LSCF), 10 g of NaCl and 10 g of KCl were mixed under ball milling for 1 h. Then, the oxides with the corresponding molar ratio (La2O3/SrCO3/Co3O4/Fe3O4 = 0.6:0.4:0.8:0.2) were added and mixed. Subsequently, the mixed powder was dried at 60 °C for 2 h and then was annealed at 850 °C for 5 h in air. When cooled to room temperature, the obtained sample (La0.6Sr0.4Co0.8Fe0.2O3−δ) was taken out and ground, which was termed A-LSCF0. The sample Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3−δ (A-BSCF0) was synthesized under the same conditions, except that BaCO3 was used instead of La2O3.

The 3D self-branched ZnCo2O4@NC hollow nanowall arrays were fabricated by carbonization-oxidation process of ZnCo-MOF precursors. A piece of ZnCo-MOF/CTs was annealed in Ar at 700 °C for 2 h with a ramp rate of 1.5 °C min−1, after cooling down to room temperature, and then the sample was once again annealed at 250 °C for 1.5 h under air atmosphere with a heating rate of 1.5 °C min−1. The mass loading of ZnCo2O4@NC was about 1.12 mg cm−2. For comparison, the 3D self-branched ZnCo2O4 growth on CTs were obtained using oxidation process of ZnCo-MOF/CTs, which was annealed in air at 400 °C for 2 h with a ramp rate of 1.5 °C min−1. The mass loading of ZnCo2O4 was around 0.896 mg cm−2. Moreover, the 2D Co3O4@NC nanowall arrays growth on CTs was also synthesized by same procedure with ZnCo2O4@NC/CTs except that of ZnCo-MOF/CTs instead of Co-MOF/CTs. The mass loading of porous Co3O4@NC was about 1.15 mg cm−2.

Synthesis of I-BSCF: In a typical preparation of I-BSCF, the as-prepared BSCF bulks (7.5 g) and I2 powders (0.5 g) were put into a specially designed quartz tube. Subsequently, the quartz tube was evacuated and sealed using an oxy-acetylene kit. After that, a calcination treatment was carried out with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 up to a target temperature (900, 1000, and 1100 °C) and kept for several hours (3, 6, and 9 h) in a muffle furnace. For simplicity, the as-prepared I-BSCF samples that synthesized under different temperatures of 900 °C, 1000 °C and 1100 °C for 6 h were termed as I-BSCF-900, I-BSCF-1000, and I-BSCF-1100. The as-prepared I-BSCF samples that synthesized under 1000 °C for 3, 6, and 9 h were termed as I-BSCF-3, I-BSCF-6, and I-BSCF-9, respectively. Finally, these products were washed with ethanol several times to remove extra I2 and dried in an oven at 70 °C. The obtained perovskite powders were finely ground using a mortar and pestle prior to further characterization.

Synthesis of c-BSCF: The as-prepared BSCF bulks without the addition of I2 powders were put into a specially designed quartz tube. Subsequently, the quartz tube was evacuated and sealed using an oxy-acetylene kit. After that, a calcination treatment was carried out with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 up to 1000 °C and kept for 6 h in a muffle furnace. Finally, the product was washed with ethanol several times to remove extra I2 and dried in an oven at 70 °C. The obtained powders were finely ground using a mortar and pestle prior to further characterization.

Synthesis of I-SNO and I-SCO: The as-prepared SNO or SCO bulks (7.5 g) and I2 powders (0.5 g) were put into a specially designed quartz tube. Subsequently, the quartz tubes were evacuated and sealed using an oxy-acetylene kit. After that, a calcination treatment was carried out with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 up to a target temperature of 900 °C and kept for 6 h in a muffle furnace. Finally, the product was washed with ethanol several times to remove extra I2 and dried in an oven at 70 °C. The obtained perovskite powders were finely ground using a mortar and pestle prior to further characterization.

SNCF powder was synthesized through a traditional solid-state reaction route. Stoichiometric amounts of SrCO3, Nb2O5, Co2O3 and Fe2O3 were weighed, mixed and milled in a planetary ball miller using ethanol as a solvent medium for 12 h. After drying, the obtained mixture were calcined at 1200 °C in air for 10 h to form perovskite structure. The calcined SNCF powder was refined by further ball-milling for 24 h, and the obtained SNCF was named as SNCF-0h. 0.5 g of SNCF-0h was added into the (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2 saturated solution (2 mL) in an airtight container. The (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2 treatment was carried out at room temperature for a time length of 6, 12, 24 and 48 h. The obtained mixture was filtered with a Buchner funnel, washed with deionized water for 10 min, and then dried in oven at 60 °C. The treated samples were named as SNCF-6h, SNCF-12h, SNCF-24h and SNCF-48h.

For preparing Co3O4/CeO2 HSs, the same mass of prepared Ce-based coordination polymer precursors and Co(CH3COO)2·4H2O were evenly mixed, and the mixture was placed in a tubular furnace as well as annealed in the air at 600 °C for 120 min to generate Co3O4/CeO2 HSs. By replacing Co(CH3COO)2·4H2O with Ni(CH3COO)2·4H2O, NiO/CeO2 HSs can be obtained under similar conditions. For comparison, the Ce-based coordination polymer NSs, Co(CH3COO)2.4H2O and Ni(CH3COO)2.4H2O were directly annealed under the same condition to yield CeO2 NFs, Co3O4 NFs and NiO NFs, respectively.

The solid-state preparation method was applied to synthesize the catalysts, including SrCoO3-δ (SC), BaCoO3-δ (BC), LaCoO3-δ (BC), La0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ (LSCF), Ba0.5Sr0.5Co0.8Fe0.2O3-δ (LSCF), SrNb0.1Ta0.1Co0.8O3-δ (SNTC) and SrSc0.175Nb0.025Co0.8O3-δ (SSNC). Stoichiometric mixtures of Co3O4 (Aldrich, ≥99.5 %), SrCO3 (Aldrich, ≥99.9 %), BaCoO3 (Aldrich, ≥99.98 %), La2O3 (Aldrich, ≥99.9 %), Nb2O5 (Aldrich, ≥99.99 %) and Ta2O5 (Alfa Aesar, ≥99.0 %) were weighed and ball-milled at 260 rpm for 20 h. Then the samples were dry-pressed in a die under 90 MPa and sintered at 1200 °C for 20 h. Finally, the sintered tablets were crushed into powders through ball-milling at 350 rpm for 8 h. As the SC powders were prepared via solid-state method and ground through ball-milling, their sizes may vary widely. Thus, the as-prepared SC powders were dispersed in ethanol via ultrasonication for 1 h, and centrifuged at the rotation speed of 1000 rpm. The supernatant was collected and dried under vacuum overnight to obtain the SC-origin with monodispersed particle size.

Copper delafossite samples were prepared using a conventional solid-state reaction. The stoichiometric monoxide precursors were mixed in agate mortar. Each precursor was pressed at 40 MPa into a pellet form and calcined at above 1000 °C for 10 h. CuYO2 was obtained by 2-step calcination . The calcined pellets were ground in agate mortar to obtain a powder of Cu delafossite compounds. Details of the synthesis are shown in Table 1.

Ba4MIr3O12 (M = Pr, Bi, Nb) perovskite oxides were prepared by solid reaction method in air. BaCO3, Pr6O11, Bi2O3, Nb2O5 and Ir were used as starting materials. To synthesize Ba4PrIr3O12, Ba4NbIr3O12 and Ba2PrIrO6, stoichiometric quantities of above materials were thoroughly grounded in an agate mortar and pelleted. The mixture was annealed first at 800 °C for 12 h and then 1150 °C for 24 h with intermediate grindings and pelletizings. To synthesize Ba4BiIr3O12, a 10% excess Bi2O3 was used to compensate for the possible evaporation. The sample was annealed at 600 °C for 12 h and then calcined at 900 °C for 24 h with intermediate grindings and pelletings.

La2LiIrO6 and La2LiRuO6 were synthesized by solid-state reaction of the precursors La2O3 (Alfa Aesar 99.99%) previously dried at 1,000 ∘C overnight, Li2CO3 (Sigma-Aldrich > 99%), IrO2 (Alfa Aesar 99%) and RuO2 (Alfa Aesar 99.9%), respectively, and fired at 900 ∘C under O2 for 24 h with intermediate grinding for La2LiIrO6 and 980 ∘C for 24 h with intermediate grinding for La2LiRuO6. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded by means of a BRUKER D8 Advance diffractometer using Cu-Kα radiation. The crystallographic parameters were determined by means of the Le Bail method using the FullProf suite.

Na2CoP2O7 and NaCoPO4 powders were synthesized using the conventional solid-state method. Stoichiometric amounts of Na2CO3 (ACS reagent, ⩾99%, Aldrich), CoC2O4 (⩾99%, Kojundo) and (NH4)2HPO4 (ACS reagent 98%, Aldrich) were wet-ball milled in acetone media and heated at 300 °C under Ar media for 6 h. After grinding, the pelletized samples were calcined at 600 °C and 750 °C under Ar media for 6 h for Na2CoP2O7 and NaCoPO4, respectively. Li2CoP2O7 and LiCoPO4 powders were synthesized using the stoichiometric amounts of Li2CO3 (ACS reagent, ⩾99%, Aldrich), CoC2O4 (⩾99%, Kojundo) and (NH4)2HPO4 (ACS reagent 98%, Aldrich). The precursors were mixed by ball milling, heated at 300 °C under Ar media for 6 h, pelletized and calcined at 600 °C for Li2CoP2O7 and 800 °C for LiCoPO4 under Ar media for 6 h. Amorphous Co-Pi was synthesized according to the previous report.

Si-incorporated perovskite samples were prepared by a ball-milling-assisted solid-state reaction approach. Freshly dried chemicals of SrCO3, Co3O4 and SiO2 (Sigma-Aldrich) were weighted according to the stoichiometric ratio of SrCo1–ySiyO3–δ (y = 0.00, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07 and 0.10) with different intentional Si-doping levels. The precursory powders were then mixed in an acetone medium for 1 h using a high-energy ball mill (Planetary Mono Mill, Pulverisette 6, Fritisch) at a rotation of 400 rpm. The as-obtained mixtures were dried, pressed into pellets and subjected to calcination in air under ambient pressure at 1000–1200 °C for 24 h with intermediate grindings. The actual composition of each sample after calcination was analysed by XRD (Supplementary Tables 1 and 2).

All composite oxides were synthesized with solid state method. Powders of SrCO3, IrO2, TiO2, and Co3O4 from Sigma-Aldrich Corporation were used as raw materials. Briefly, stoichiometry amount of raw materials were weighted and then mixed in mortar. The mixed precursors were finally calcined with box furnaces in ambient air. A sintering condition of 850 °C for 12 h, 1100 °C for 12° h, and 1200 °C for 12 h is applied for m-SrIrO3, SrCo0.9Ir0.1O3−δ, and SrCo0.9Ti0.1O3−δ respectively.

The (FexNi1−x)4N NPs were obtained by calcination. First of all, the as-prepared Ni-doped iron oxide precursor was ground into powder, 0.4 g of the precursor was put into a porcelain boat and placed in the center of the quartz tube in a tubular furnace. Another porcelain boat with 1.5 mL of ethylenediamine was placed 3 cm in front of the boat containing the precursor. Next, under the protection of N2, the tube furnace was heated to 560–660 °C at a rate of 20 °C min−1 and then held at the final temperature for 30 min, then it was naturally cooled to room temperature (RT). Finally, black powder products were collected after drying, washing and grinding.

The Ni1−xLixO mixed oxides were prepared as described elsewhere through solid state reactions of oxidation–decomposition. Appropriate amounts of the precursors: Ni (99.99%, Aldrich) and Li2CO3 (99.9%, Aldrich) pellets, were mixed and mechanically activated by ball milling before the thermal treatment, which was performed in air at 850 °C for 12 h. Cylinders and disks were prepared by compressing (∼200 MPa) the powders in a mold (diameter 1 cm) and then sintering the compacted material for 4 h at 800 °C in air.

ZIF-67 and ruthenium acetylacetonate (3 : 1 weight ratio, respectively) were taken and ground well in a mortar-pestle. The obtained powder was added into a closed quartz tube and subjected to pyrolysis at 900 °C for 2 h under a nitrogen atmosphere in a Lenton tube furnace. The as-obtained black product was named as RuCo@NC. Palladium acetylacetonate and platinum acetylacetonate were used in place of ruthenium acetylacetonate to obtain PdCo@NC and PtCo@NC, respectively.

(i) The polycrystalline sample of Na4Co3P4O15 (SS) was synthesized using a conventional solid-state reaction. A starting mixture of NaPO3 and Co3O4 (4 : 1 molar ratio) was milled for 0.5 h in an agate mortar. The powder was transferred to a ceramic crucible and pre-heated at 400 °C for 2 h. Then, the powder was re-ground and finally calcined at the optimized temperature (700 °C) for 50 h.

Single crystals of FeP and m-NiP2 were grown via Sn-flux. Elements were loaded into a silica ampoule (total sample weight 50 g) in the M : P : Sn ratio of 1 : 1 : 50 for FeP and 1 : 2 : 50 for m-NiP2, evacuated, and flame sealed. The ampoule was heated from room-temperature to 1373 K over 17 h, annealed for 48 h, and cooled to 773 K over 120 h. At 773 K the ampoule was flipped and centrifuged to remove most of the molten tin. After centrifugation, the samples were treated with a 1 : 1 HCl : H2O solution for 5 h, filtered, and dried. Large shiny, silvery crystals were collected (Fig. 1).

Single crystals of PtSn4 were synthesized by the self-flux method. High-purity Pt foil (99.99%) and Sn ingots (99.999%) were mixed in a ratio of 1 : 25 and sealed in an evacuated quartz tube with a flat bottom. The mixed elements were heated to 600 °C for 6 hours, kept for 10 hours, quickly cooled to 350 °C in 5 hours, and then slowly cooled at a rate of 1°C h−1 to 250 °C. Subsequently, the excess Sn flux was removed by centrifugation and then etched in concentrated hydrochloric acid. The PtSn4 crystals were cleaved in ultra-high vacuum (UHV) by the post-method with the natural cleavage plane coinciding with the (010) orientation. Surface cleanliness was checked by XPS (ESI, Fig. S2†) and vibrational spectroscopy, while surface crystalline order was checked by LEED and STM.

AlFe2B2 was synthesized as previously reported. Starting materials were mixed in a Al : Fe : B = 3 : 2 : 2 ratio (a total weight of 0.35 g) and pressed into a pellet, which was arc-melted in an argon-filled glovebox. The pellet was re-melted 4 times to achieve uniform melting. To maximize the sample's homogeneity, it was sealed in a silica tube under vacuum (∼10−5 Torr) and annealed at 900 °C for 1 week. The annealed sample was ground and subjected to powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), which revealed AlFe2B2 as the major phase and Al13F4 as a minor byproduct. The latter was removed by treating the sample for 3–5 min with dilute (1 : 1 v/v) aqueous solution of HCl.

Li2CO3, NiO and MnO were mixed in a stoichiometric ratio of 0.63 : 0.2 : 0.6 and ground in a mortar for 30 min. The obtained powder was then calcined at 450 °C for 5 h (heating rate: 2.0 °C min−1) and at 850 °C for 15 h (heating rate: 2.0 °C min−1) in air to get the final Li1.2Ni0.2Mn0.6O2.

Na2CO3 and MnO with a stoichiometric ratio of 0.352 : 1 were mixed and ground in a mortar for 30 min. The obtained powder was then calcined at 450 °C for 5 h (heating rate: 2.0 °C min−1) and at 850 °C for 15 h (heating rate: 2.0 °C min−1) in air to get the final Na0.67MnO2.

The bulk SNCF catalyst was prepared by a traditional solid-state reaction method. Briefly, stoichiometric amounts of SrCO3, Nb2O5, Co2O3, and Fe2O3 were weighed, mixed and milled in a planetary mill (Fritsch, Pulverisette 6) using acetone as a solvent medium at 400 rpm for 1 h. The homogeneously dispersed mixture was then dried and calcined at 1200 °C in air for 10 h to form the bulk SNCF powder. The SCF used in this study was also prepared by the solid-state reaction method and the precursor mixture was calcined at 1100 °C in air for 5 h. The commercial IrO2 (for OER) and 20 wt% Pt/C (for HER) catalysts used in this study were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation and Johnson Matthey Company, respectively.

The SCS perovskite catalyst was prepared using the self-assembly solid-state reaction. The stoichiometric amounts of strontium carbonate (SrCO3, 99%), cobalt oxide (Co2O3, 99.9%), and stannic oxide (SnO2, 99.5%) were mixed with ethanol and ball-milled in a planetary ball miller for 5 hours at 450 rpm, followed by drying in air to acquire a solid precursor. The obtained precursor was subsequently fired at 1100°C for 10 hours in air with a 5°C min−1 heating rate to obtain the desired perovskite catalyst. The resulting catalyst was further ball-milled for 10 hours at 500 rpm for further characterization and testing.

Layered KC powder was synthesized from Co(NO3)2·6H2O mixed with KOH (99.99%, Aladdin) in the molar ratio of 1:10 in a nickel crucible. The mixture was immediately transferred to a muffle furnace with 600 °C for 12 h. The heating rate was controlled at 2 °C min−1 to form the molten KOH. The products were washed with ultrapure water to remove excess alkali and put into a 65 °C drying oven for 12 h.

The obtained 20 mg pre-CoPc@CNT and 400 mg sodium hypophosphite were ground together and then calcined at 300 °C for 2 h under Ar gas conditions at a heating rate of 2 °C min−1. The mixture was etched by 3 m of HCl for 12 h and then washed by water for three times to obtain the 5.6%P-CoPc@CNT. The 6.6%P-CoPc@CNT and 7.8%P-CoPc@CNT were similarly prepared by following this procedure except for changing the content of sodium hypophosphite into 0.8 and 1.2 g, respectively.

A solid-state method was used to synthesize CaCu3Ru4O12. CaCO3, CuO, and RuO2 were mixed as precursors in a stoichiometric ratio of 1:3:4 and pressed into pellets. The pellets were then placed in a furnace and heated at 950 °C under ambient pressure for 48 h. After heating, the pellets were thoroughly ground into powder to obtain the catalyst powder.

CeCu3Ru4O12 was synthesized using a high-temperature, high-pressure method. CeO2, CuO, Cu2O, and RuO2 were mixed at a molar ratio of 1:1:1:4 and pressed into pellets. The pellets were heated at 1100 °C and 12 GPa for 30 min, followed by being quenched to room temperature (25 °C) and then decompressed to room pressure. Finally, the pellets were ground thoroughly to obtain the final catalyst powder.

KMoS2 was synthesized via the high temperature solid-state reactions. K2S2 powder, Mo powder, and MoS2 powder were mixed and ground thoroughly in a mortar. After the mixture was pressed into a pellet and sealed in the evacuated quartz tube, it was transferred into muffle furnace and annealed at 850 °C for 20 h. Then the obtained sample was naturally cooled to room temperature.

The bulk CoPS was synthesized through a solid state reaction method. In brief, the mixtures powders of cobalt, sulfur and red phosphorus were put into quartz tube and then sealed in a vacuum. Next, the tube was heated to 1000 °C and kept for 2 days and afterward cooled down to room temperature naturally.

Ruddlesden–Popper powders were prepared by conventional solid-state reaction but with a novel approach starting from a stoichiometric mixture of Pr0.5Ca0.5MnO3 and CaO powders. The reagents were mixed in an agate mortar, ball-milled for 15 min, and heated in air at 1100 °C for 24 h. Structural analysis by X-ray diffraction and Rietveld refinement has already been published.

An aluminum foil was sonicated in ethanol for 30 s to remove organic contaminants from its surface. A fresh solution of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (Sigma-Aldrich, >98%), Co(NO3)3·9H2O (Sigma-Aldrich, >98%), and Al(NO3)3·9H2O (Sigma-Aldrich, ≥99%) was prepared with concentrations of 0.05 M for both salts of Fe and Co and 0.1 M for the Al salt, by using anhydrous ethanol (Fluka, 99.8%). The solution was deposited on the aluminum foil by drop casting and dried at room temperature. Afterward, the substrate was introduced into a horizontal quartz tube CVD reactor flooded with helium [200 standard cubic centimeters per minute (sccm)] and hydrogen (45 sccm) and heated to 645 °C over 2 h (heating rate of 5 °C min–1). After the mixture had reached the reaction temperature, acetylene (12 sccm) as the carbon source was added to the gas stream for 20 min. After the reaction time, the reactor was cooled to room temperature under a He/H2 flow. The so-deposited aluminum foil was removed from the reactor and characterized. The multiwall carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) have a diameter of ∼20 nm and a length of 35 ± 3 μm.

For phosphorization, 500 mg of sodium hypophosphite (NaH2PO2·H2O) and SSM-Ni were put at two separate positions in a porcelain boat with NaH2PO2·H2O at the upstream side of the three-temperature zone tube furnace and the SSM-Ni at the second-temperature zone. The sample was heated at 400 °C for 60 min with a heating rate of 5 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere with a flow rate of 200 sccm. After natural cooling, the SSM-Ni-P sample was obtained.

Thin Film Electrode Preparation. Thin films were prepared by magnetron sputter deposition onto Ti disks (6 mm diameter, 4 mm thick, ACI alloys, 99.99% pure) in a sputter deposition chamber (AJA International, Inc.) with a nominal base pressure of 4 × 10–8 Pa equipped with both radio frequency (RF) and direct current (DC) power supplies. Prior to deposition of metal films, the substrates were cleaned with RF Ar plasma for 4 min (25 W RF, 0.2 Pa Ar). As-deposited films were removed from the sputter chamber protected by a drop of Millipore water and transferred to a tube furnace (Carbolite) where the films were annealed under controlled atmosphere or stored for XAS experiments. For XAS experiments, the samples were sputtered to the 1–3 nm thickness (see Supporting Information for details). Annealed films were produced by annealing in a tube furnace for 1 h at 350 °C in a Ar-3%H2; these conditions were found to elicit the same surface enrichment without affecting the Ti substrate.

Solid solution NiMoO4 thin films were fabricated on NF substrates using AACVD as shown in Scheme 1. The feedstock solution was prepared prior to the AACVD process. The details of the setup and working principle of AACVD is also elaborated in our recent work.  Briefly, the precursors, 100 mg (0.4 mmol) of Ni(acac)2 and 126 mg (0.4 mmol) of MoO2(acac)2, were dissolved in 20 mL of methanol at room temperature and the solution was stirred continuously. The yellowish green solution obtained after 30 min of stirring acted as a feedstock for thin film deposition. Films were prepared by altering the deposition time for 60 and 120 min at a fixed temperature of 480 °C. The liquefied precursor was then converted into a gaseous stream with the help of an ultrasonic generator and delivered to a reactor tube, fitted in a horizontal tube furnace, with the aid of carrier gas (N2, 99.99% purity). The temperature of the tube furnace was set at 480 °C, and the NF substrate was positioned in such a way that the precursor mist directly landed on its surface. The precursor cloud decomposed on the heated NF surface to form thin films. The AACVD process for different time periods was carried out to obtain samples with different microstructures. After completing the deposition process, samples were cooled under the flow of N2 gas until the furnace temperature reached 50 °C. The gray films obtained after 60 and 120 min of deposition were designated as NM1 and NM2, respectively.

The MXene were synthesized by etching the Ti3AlC2 MAX phase with HF, as reported elsewhere.  For the synthesis of fluffy 3D MXene architectures, the Ti3C2 MXene colloid with certain concentration (0.5–5 mg mL–1) was ultrasonically nebulized to aerosol droplets by an ultrasonic atomizer (3.2 W, Siansonic DP30) with a feeding rate of 20 mL h–1. The aerosol droplets were subsequently flowed through a tube furnace connected to the ultrasonic atomizer with Ar as carrier gas. The tube furnace was preheated at 600 °C before aerosolization. The sphere-like 3D MXene architectures were produced by similar way except to add the polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, 2 mg mL–1) in MXene colloids. The products were harvested by an electrostatic collector at the end of tube furnace.

The as-obtained Ni-Co3O4 powders were placed into the center of a tubular furnace, and 0.4000 g of Se powder was placed at the upstream side of the furnace. Then, the furnace was rapidly heated up to 400 °C under an N2 atmosphere and kept at these conditions for 3 h. The as-obtained product was denoted as Se/Ni-Co3O4-0.4. The Se powder dosage (0.1000, 0.2000, 0.8000, and 1.2000 g) was further investigated to study its effect on the catalytic activity of the as synthesized samples, denoted as Se/Ni-Co3O4-0.1, Se/Ni-Co3O4-0.2, Se/Ni-Co3O4-0.8, and Se/Ni-Co3O4-1.2, respectively. For the comparison reason, Se-Co3O4-0.1, Se-Co3O4-0.2, Se-Co3O4-0.4, Se-Co3O4-0.8, and Se/Ni-Co3O4-1.2 samples were also prepared with the same procedure. Their detailed contents (atomic percent) were characterized by energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) (Table S1, Table S2).

For the synthesis of CoTe2@N-GC, 100 mg of ZIF-67 and 300 mg of Te powder were placed at two separate porcelain boats in a tube furnace and were heated at 450 °C for 6 h under a combination atmosphere of 5% H2 and 95% Ar. For the synthesis of CoTe2 powder, 100 mg of ZIF-67 was first annealed at 450 °C for 6 h under an air atmosphere to get the Co3O4 precursor before undergoing the same chemical conversion process with Te powder in separate porcelain boats.

NiCo-CH NW/NF and α-Ni(OH)2 NS/NF were transformed into NiCoP NW/NF and Ni2P NS/NF, respectively, through a low-temperature phosphorization procedure in a tube furnace. In detail, a porcelain boat filled with 500 mg of NaH2PO2 was placed at the upstream position of the tube furnace, while the sample was placed at the downstream position (the center of the heat source). Subsequently, the sample was heated at 300 °C with a slow heating rate of 2 °C min–1 and maintained for 2 h with continuous argon gas flow, and then cooled naturally to ambient temperature.

Ir thin films were deposited in a traveling-wave-type ALD reactor (NCD Technology, Lucida D100, Korea) using the TICP precursor and O2 (99.99%). TICP was synthesized by TANAKA Precious Metals (Japan) by a previously reported process.  The range of deposition temperature was 200–325 °C. A standard cycle of the Ir ALD process consisted of precursor pulsing for 7 s, reactant pulsing for 5 s, and purging for 10 s. This process was established according to the self-limiting growth criteria discussed below. The precursor was heated at 55 °C to obtain a suitable vapor pressure during film deposition. N2 (100 sccm) was used as the carrier and purge gas for the metal precursor and was also used between two consecutive pulses during deposition. Deposition was typically conducted on H-terminated Si substrates. Furthermore, the growth behavior on the following surfaces was also investigated: SiO2 (100 nm), sputtered TiN (thickness: 20 nm on Si), a Ru film grown by ALD using tricarbonyl(trimethylenemethane)ruthenium [Ru(TMM) (CO)3] as a precursor and O2 as a reactant (thickness: 10 nm on Si),  and Al2O3 obtained by ALD using trimethylaluminum [Al(CH3)3] as the precursor and H2O as the reactant (thickness: 10 nm/Si).  The Ru film also acted as the seed layer for Ir ALD in some experiments.

The oxidation state of the nickel-based deposits was controlled by adjusting the gas mixture used for the ignition of the magnetron plasma discharge during film growth. Thus, metallic nickel Ni, nickel oxide NiO, or nickel oxyhydroxide NiOx(OH)y films were produced with Ar or an Ar/O2 or Ar/O2/H2O plasma gas mixtures, respectively. Ar and O2 gases were supplied to the preparation chamber by mass flow controllers (30 sccm of Ar for metallic, 15 sccm of Ar and 15 sccm of O2 for oxide, and 15 sccm of Ar and 4 sccm of O2 for hydroxide samples, respectively), while water in the gas mixture was controlled with a leak valve adjusted to dose a constant partial pressure of 5 × 10–4 mbar in the deposition chamber. In all cases, total deposition pressure was fixed at 5 × 10–3 mbar (base pressure of the system was 2 × 10–6 mbar).

To synthesize the CoFeS (Se or P) frame/cagelike structure, the CoFe PBA precursor was annealed under different S, Se, and P vapors. Briefly, 50 mg of CoFe PBA and 1 g of sulfur (500 mg of selenium or 1 g of sodium hypophosphite) powder were put separately into two porcelain boats at the upstream side of the tube furnace under Ar gas. The CoFeS (Se or P) frame/cagelike structures were acquired by the annealing process in two steps. First, the tube furnace was heated at 150 °C for 1 h with a heating rate of 2 °C min–1, and then the temperature was increased to 350 °C with a heating rate of 3 °C min–1 and was kept for 3.30 h.

20 mg CoCPNFs and 1 g NaH2PO2·H2O were placed at both ends of the ceramic boat. Then, the porcelain boat is placed in a tubular furnace and the porcelain boat containing NaH2PO2·H2O is located at upstream. Whereafter, the tube furnace was heated to 350 °C at a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 and maintained for 2 h under argon protection. The obtained black powder is denoted as CoP@NCNFs. Furthermore, in order to investigate the effect of different phosphorylation amounts on the catalytic activity, CoP@NCNFs-0.5 and CoP@NCNFs-1.5 were also prepared with the similar steps except the addition amount of sodium hypophosphite during phosphorylation (Table S1).

The as-prepared NiCo-LDH/Ni foam and 100 mg selenium powder were loaded into a ceramic boat and the selenium powder was placed at the upstream of the ceramic boat. Subsequently, the samples were placed at the center of the tube furnace. The sample was then heated at 450 °C for 2 h under the protection of Ar gas with a heating rate of 5 °C/min. As a control, we also prepared a sample with 200 mg of Se powder.

The iron oxide deposited on commercial Ni foam was carried out by the ALD technique (Ensure Scientific). During the deposition process, ferrocene and O3 were used as the iron and oxygen source, respectively. The reactor and the ferrocene source were maintained at 250 and 80 °C, respectively. For each ALD cycle, the pulse time and purging time for ferrocene and O3 were 1 s/15 s and 0.5 s/8 s, respectively. After that, the nickel foam with iron oxide and NaH2PO2·H2O were placed separately in a porcelain boat. Then, the sample was heated to 300 °C under the flowing nitrogen atmosphere for 2 h and then cooled to ambient temperature.

Nickel foams with dimensions of 1 × 0.12 × 4 cm3 were ultrasonically cleaned in acetone, ethanol, and water for 10 min, sequentially. After drying, the foams were placed into a quartz tube furnace for CVD growth of graphene nanosheets. The samples were heated to 1080 °C with a heating rate of 15 °C.min−1 under 45.5 sccm Ar/H2 gas flow (9:1 vol/vol). After annealing for 15 min, 20 sccm CH4 gas was blown for 15 min. Afterward, the furnace was turned off and the feeding of methane gas was stopped. The specimens were taken outside of the heating zone of the furnace and cooled under the Ar/H2 gas flow.

The Ta3N5 films grown on tantalum foil was synthesized from Ta3O5 films by a single chemical vapor deposition (CVD) step. Nanoporous tantalum oxide films were a porcelain boat at the middle side of the furnace. The porcelain boat was heated to 800 °C for 1 h under a mixture of argon (Flow rate is 50 sccm) and ammonia (Flow rate is 20 sccm) atmosphere at a total pressure of 0.35 Kpa, and then was cooled to room temperature. The temperature increasing rate is 10 °C min−1.

We use a chemical vapor deposition (CVD) method developed in our lab to prepare N-doped CNTs on nickel foam. The original nickel foam will become brittle after annealing, which is unfavorable for acting as a substrate material. After a series of trial and error, we found that the pressed nickel foam can sustain its ductility after annealing. Thus, all the nickel foam were pressed before using. Before the deposition process, the nickel foam were cut into 1 cm × 1.5 cm squares and pressed to 0.16 mm thickness, and then put into a 6 M HCl solution to sonicate for 30 min. After that the nickel foam were rinsed with copious deionized water (DI) and ethanol to remove the residue impurity. The obtained nickel foam were put in an electronic vacuum oven for 12 h to vapor the trace amount of liquid. Following that, the nickel foam were used as the substrate to load N-doped CNTs. Briefly, the nickel foam were placed in the middle of a quartz tube. The system was purged with high purity argon gas with a flow rate of 800 sccm for 30 min to ensure an inert environment. After that the furnace was heated in a programmable step to 900 °C in 30 min and then kept at this temperature for another 15 min. Ferrocene, ethylene gas and melamine which acts as the catalyst, carbon source and nitrogen source, respectively, was introduced into the tube lasting for 15 min to deposition N-doped CNTs. Finally after turning down the ethylene gas, the furnace was left to cool down to room temperature, during which the argon gas was continue purged to protect the obtained material. The obtained material was donated as NCNTs@NF.

Co NPs@CNT was prepared by chemical vapor deposition with using commercial Co (99%, Aladdin) and benzene (99.5%, Aladdin) as precursors. In brief, 500 mg of commercial Co were placed in a crucible with benzene and placed in a tube furnace under flowing Ar. At 600 °C, Co NPs@CNT was formed in situ and then dried in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 8 h.

Atomic layer deposition (ALD): The core-shell structure was successfully synthesized using atomic layer deposition (ALD). The as-synthesized nano-rod on NF was used as a substrate without any treatment for growing the core-shell structure. For a typical synthesis of the core-shell, cobalt oxide thin films were deposited around of 200 atomic layer deposition systems. Highly pure Nitrogen used as an inert environment cobalt (2+); (C-methyl-N-propan-2-ylcarbonimidoyl)-propan-2-ylazanide, precursors were used as a cobalt source and ultra-pure H2O as an oxidation agent. The cobalt precursor remained heated up to 115 °C, and finally, its deposition remains at 150 °C temperature. The Co3O4 thickness layer is controlled by modifying the no. of cycles ALD. Subsequent pulsing cycles of 1s for cobalt precursor, 0.015 s for water precursor with 20 cm pure nitrogen flow were used. For 2000 cycles 20 nm (S1), 30 nm (S2), and 40 nm (S3) thick film for all three prepared electro-catalysts were deposited on NiMoS2 nano-rods (S1, S2 and S3). The combined core-shell structure annealed at 300 °C for 5 h in the box furnace. The exact mass of the Ni foam nano-sheets is resolute by deducting the exact mass deposition before and after. The loading densities of the reaction active nanocomposite materials remained almost 4.20 mg/cm.

Sodium hypophosphite (NaH2PO2·H2O, 0.3 g), as the P source, was placed upstream of porcelain coat, and NHEMH was placed downstream. Before chemical vapour deposition (CVD), the tube furnace was constantly filled with N2 for 30 min. The heating rate was 5 ℃/min in a 100 mL/min N2 stream. Then, the skeleton was heated at 350 ℃ for 2 h. After heating, the furnace was cooled down to room temperature. To achieve a high-activity bifunctional electrocatalyst, we choose a heating rate of 2 ℃/min, 5 ℃/min, and 10 ℃/min and a NaH2PO2·H2O masses of 0.1 g, 0.2 g, 0.3 g, and 0.4 g as the test parameters. It is well known that the self-supported electrodes, such as nickel foam (≈$2.33 for 1 g) and carbon paper (≈$5.46 for 1 g) etc., are widely applied. However, the cost of PNHEMH is ≈$0.73 for 1 g, which is approximately 1/3 of nickle foam (NF) and 1/7 of carbon paper (CP), respectively. Moreover, the cost of PNHEMH is only ≈1/130 of IrO2 and Pt/C (≈$95 for 1 g) electrocatalysts at the same mass.

The P doping was realized based on the reduction of oxides by phosphine (PH3) generated from the pyrolysis of sodium hypophosphite (NaH2PO2·H2O). Specifically, NaH2PO2·H2O and the oxides (mass ratio = 10 : 1) were separately placed in a porcelain boat with NaH2PO2·H2O upstream in the tube furnace. The furnace was heated from room temperature to 350 °C for 2 h with a ramping rate of 2.5 °C min−1 under N2 atmosphere. The P-doped oxides, respectively denoted as P-MC, P-NMC and P-ZNMC, were acquired after cooling down to room temperature.

NaH2PO2·H2O (1 g) was placed upstream of a tube furnace, and the obtained FNM/NF-1 (1.7 cm × 1.5 cm) was placed downstream. After that, a continuous flow of inert gas was maintained in the tube furnace, and the temperature was raised to 300 °C for 2 h at a heating rate of 3 °C per minute to finally obtain (Fe0.1Ni0.9)2P(O)/NF. Ni2P(O)/NF and (Fe0.2Ni0.8)2P(O)/NF were obtained by the same phosphating method using the FNM/NF-0 and FNM/NF-2 precursors, respectively.

In a typical procedure, the as-prepared FeOOH@Ni3Si4O10(OH)2·5H2O NSs and commercial NaH2PO2 (mass ratio of 1 : 5) were loaded into a porcelain crucible and placed at two separate positions, respectively, keeping the NaH2PO2 at the upstream side of the tube furnace. The phosphorization treatment was performed in a N2 (99.999%) atmosphere, in which the sample was heated to 500 °C at a rate of 2 °C min−1, maintained at this temperature for 2 h, and finally cooled down to room temperature naturally. The as-prepared product was immersed in 2 M NaOH for 1 h to remove redundant SiO2.

An alumina boat containing 0.4 g of Co2SiO4 powder was loaded into a quartz tube inside chemical vapour deposition (CVD) system. The quartz tube was evacuated to remove the air via vacuum pump and then purged and refilled with a 10% H2/Ar gas mixture till the pressure reached ∼105 Pa. After reaching the target pressure, the CVD temperature was raised to 800 °C for 2 h and kept at this temperature for 1.5 h. This thermal reduction alters Co2SiO4 to form Co@SiO2 structures. After the process at 800 °C, inlet was switched to a C2H2 gas with a flow of 40 sccm for 10 min at the same temperature and then, the treated product was allowed to cool down naturally under a flow of 30% H2/Ar mixture gas. This process generates a Co@SG@SiO2 structure, which has a single-layer graphene shell at the interface between Co NP and SiO2. After the achievement of this result, the as-prepared Co@SG@SiO2 was etched in 5.0 M KOH solution at 90 °C for 6 h to remove SiO2. Subsequently, the solid product was filtered and washed several times with plenty of DI water and ethanol and then dried at 70 °C overnight in an electric oven to have Co@SG nanoparticles (NPs).

The series of copper cobalt sulfides were synthesized by thermal annealing of Cu0.33Co0.67(OH)2 precursor powders with sulfur powders (Aladdin, 99.95% metals basis). For the preparation of Cu0.33Co67S2, 50 mg Cu0.33Co0.67(OH)2 precursor powders and 100 mg sulfur powders were separately put on opposite ends of a quartz boat in CVD furnace, which was keeping at 250 °C for 3 h with the heating rate of 2 °C min−1 under a H2/Ar atmosphere, and then cooled down naturally to obtain the black power of Cu0.33Co67S2. Similarly, CuCo2S4 and Cu3Co6S8 can be synthetized at the temperature of 300 °C, 350 °C, 400 °C and 500 °C in the same way, respectively.

For all depositions titanium (Alfa Aesar) was used as a substrate. Since the pure catalytic activity of thin film catalysts is studied, titanium was chosen with regard to its low catalytic activity towards the OER. Additional, titanium guarantees an adequate electrical conductivity. The substrates were cleaned consecutively by ultrasonication for 5 min in iso-propanol, in ethanol and in deionized water before deposition. In some cases the titanium substrates were additionally etched in 6 N HCl for 5 minutes. As precursor for CVD cobalt(iii)-acetylacetonate (Sigma Aldrich, 99.99% metal trace), denoted as Co(acac)3, was supplied to the reactor chamber with argon as the carrier gas. Simultaneously either oxygen or air was introduced by a separate supply line as the reactive gas. The precursor was evaporated at temperatures between 190–195 °C, and the substrate was heated to temperatures between 350 °C to 430 °C.

A 20 mg CNTs@Co precursor was ground into fine powder in an agate mortar and placed in a porcelain boat in the downstream of the quartz tube. Meanwhile, 300 mg NaH2PO2 was put in the upstream of the quartz tube. The furnace was heated to 350 °C with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 for 2 h under a 100 sccm Ar atmosphere, and then naturally cooled to room temperature. The final product was abbreviated as CNTs@Co/CoP300mg. For comparison, samples of different phosphidation degrees were synthesized using the same method using various amounts of NaH2PO2, including 100 mg (CNTs@Co/CoP100mg), 500 mg (CNTs@Co/CoP500mg) and 1 g (CNTs@Co/CoP1g), respectively.

The as-prepared core–shell ZIF-67@CCHH@NF was placed in the center of a fused silica tube and an alumina boat containing 0.5 g of sublimed sulfur was placed upstream. The furnace temperature was ramped to 500 °C at a rate of 3 °C min−1 and held for 2 h with N2 as the carrier gas to give CoS2@NGC@NF. The loading amount of CoS2@NGC is ∼1.03 mg cm−2.

Core–shell Co(OH)2/SFM hybrid was prepared by an atomic-layer-deposition (ALD) technology. Firstly, the ALD-ZnO film was uniformly deposited on the as-prepared SFM-NF surface by ALD. The zinc and oxygen precursors were derived from diethylzinc and deionized water, respectively. The thickness of the ZnO film was precisely controlled via the number of cycles. In the current study, an ∼10 nm thick ZnO layer was achieved by 75 cycles. Later, the resultant core–shell structured ZnO/SFM precursor was soaked in a 6 M Co(NO3)2 aqueous solution. During the immersion, Co2+ started to hydrolyze and resulted in a Co(OH)2 colloid and H+. Simultaneously, the surface ZnO was etched by H+ and the consumption of H+ accelerated the following reaction Co2+ + H2O → H+ + Co(OH)2. Finally, this hybrid was collected and dried. The core–shell structured samples with the Co(OH)2 thickness of 5 nm and 20 nm were also obtained for comparison. The optimized composite with the Co(OH)2 thickness of 10 nm, denoted as Co(OH)2/SFM-NF, was used for the detailed studies. The fabrication protocol is illustrated in Fig. 1a. For comparison, SFM powder was prepared according to a glycine-nitrate process and was represented as SFM-NP and the fabrication of Co(OH)2 nanoflakes was performed by following the procedure reported by Chen.

The Co2P/CoSe2 composite material was prepared by a simple phosphoselenization method from the Co(OH)2/NF precursor, in which NaH2PO2 was used as bonderite and Se powder was used as the selenide reagent, respectively. First, a size of 2.0 × 3.0 cm2 Co(OH)2/NF precursor, 1.0 g NaH2PO2, and 50 mg Se powder were placed in three different porcelain boats, and then transferred to a tubular furnace for calcination. The porcelain boats containing NaH2PO2 and Se powder were placed at the top of the tubular furnace and the porcelain boat containing the Co(OH)2/NF precursor was placed below them. Before calcination, nitrogen was passed through in a straight line under a 100 sccm flow of N2 gas to remove air from the tubular furnace and to ensure an oxygen-free environment during the calcination. The calcination process was performed at various temperatures (250 °C, 300 °C, and 350 °C) for an hour and a half with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1. To prepare the control group, Co2P/NF and CoSe2/NF were prepared by phosphorization and selenization of the Co(OH)2/NF precursors under similar preparation conditions to Co2P/CoSe2.

Synthesis of CoSe2 NAs/CC—To prepare CoSe2 NAs/CC, Co(OH)F NAs/CC and Se powder were put at two ends in a combustion boat, where the Se powder (200 mg) at the upstream and the Co(OH)F at the downstream of the quartz tube, and heated at 450 °C for 2 h (ramping rate: 3 °C min−1) in Ar atmosphere.

To prepare Co–Mo2C@NC/CC, the synthesized Mo-HZIF/CC was calcined at 700 °C under an Ar atmosphere (50 sccm) for 3 h at a heating speed of 5 °C min−1. Then, Co–Mo2C@NC/CC (2 cm×3 cm) was further calcined at 300 °C for 2 h in the presence of NaH2PO2 (300 mg), which was maintained upstream of the Ar flow (50 sccm) to produce CoP–Mo2C@NC/CC.

Synthesis of CoO/CoSe2 Nanocomposites on Ti Mesh: In a typical procedure, the Ti mesh with CoSe2/DETA on surface was placed in the center of the tube furnace. The furnace was heated to 450 °C with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1, and treated at this temperature for 4 h in the O2 (0.018 vol%)/Ar atmosphere. The sample was taken out of the furnace after naturally cooled to room temperature.

Preparation of LaNiO3-NCNTs: LaNiO3-NCNTs were prepared by a one-step CVD method. The as-prepared LaNiO3 (20 mg) was placed in the middle of a quartz tube. Melamine (1.5 g) as the nitrogen and carbon source was placed at the entrance of the tube. When the temperature was heated to 700 °C at a ramping rate of 20 °C min−1 under an argon flow of 900 sccm, the melamine was then decomposed and formed the nitrogen doped carbon nanotubes. After 15 min, the reaction system was turned off and cooled to room temperature. The as-synthesized LaNiO3-NCNTs were then collected for further analysis. The mass ratio of the NCNTs to LaNiO3 is about 1:1.

The procedures for preparation of the LaNiO3 nanoparticles and the LaNiO3-NCNTs composite materials are shown in Figure 1. In the first stage, amorphous LaNiO3 nanoparticles were obtained by using an oil bath method. Then, the crystalline LaNiO3 was synthesized after annealed at 700 °C for 12 h in air. Finally, the LaNiO3-NCNTs were prepared by one-step CVD method. Here, melamine was used as both the carbon and nitrogen source to grow NCNTs. Metallic nickel derived from LaNiO3 surface should play the role of catalysts for NCNTs growth, which can be further demonstrated from the obvious peaks of metallic nickel based on XRD of LaNiO3-NCNTs (Figure S1, Supporting Information).,

500 mg of sodium hypophosphite powder was supplied in the upstream furnace and 4 pieces of CLDH/CuO/CF were placed in the downstream one (See the illustration in Figure S1, Supporting Information). The sodium hypophosphite was heated at 5 °C min−1 and kept at 300 °C for 1 h. Meanwhile, the CLDH/CuO/CF was heated at 2 °C min−1 and kept at 150 °C for 1 h. The furnaces were cooled down to room temperature naturally. The CP/CF was prepared via direct phosphorization of CuO/CF. The mass loading of CP on CF was estimated to be 7.1 mg cm−2 for the CP/CF, while the total amount of CLDH/CP on CF was approximately 7.4 mg cm−2.

The NiOOH/Ni(OH)2 was deposited on PIM-CF by using a Savannah S100 ALD reactor (Ultratech Inc.). The sample was loaded in the ALD reaction chamber and heated at 140 °C. The bis(cyclopentadienyl)nickel(II) precursor was preheated to 80 °C, and O3 was used as a counter reactant. A Cambridge NanoTech Savannah Ozone generator was used to produce O3 from pure O2. Dynamic vacuum conditions were used for the uniform coating of NiOOH/Ni(OH)2 on PIM-CF. The pulse, exposure, and purge times for the bis(cyclopentadienyl)nickel(II) precursor were 1, 10, and 10 s, respectively, and for O3 1, 10, and 5 s, respectively. Prior to NiOOH/Ni(OH)2 deposition, PIM-CF were first treated with O3 (with the same conditions for one ALD cycle) to produce −OH functional groups on the surface. 100 cycles of ALD were deposited to acquire Ni@PIM-CF. The schematic representation of the experimental procedure is illustrated in Scheme 2.

As-prepared CoOx@g-C3N4 was thermally converted to Co/N-CNT hybrids in an atmosphere of H2. CoOx@g-C3N4 powder was placed at the center of a tube furnace and calcined at 400–800 °C and different H2 flow rates (100–1500 sccm) for 1 h to optimize the morphology and electrocatalytic activity of the resulting Co/N-CNT hybrids.

Co/N-CNT hybrids were synthesized in two steps using graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, produced from melamine) as a source of carbon and nitrogen to form N-CNTs encapsulating Co nanoparticles. g-C3N4 was used as a starting carbon matrix due to a high concentration of nitrogen, a high chemical stability, relatively low cost, and abundance. In the first step, 10-nm-thick CoOx layers on bulk g-C3N4 were deposited by ALD using dicarbonylcyclopentadienyl cobalt (I) (CpCo(CO)2) as a Co precursor. In the second step, Co/N-CNT hybrids were produced by annealing as-prepared CoOx/g-C3N4 at different temperatures (400–800 °C) and H2 flow rates (100–1500 sccm) (Scheme 1). Figure S1 (Supporting Information) shows that as temperature increased to 600 °C, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) peak of bulk g-C3N4 at 2θ = 27.6° was replaced by a broad peak of the (002) plane of graphitic carbon layers at 2θ ≈ 26°, indicating the complete reduction of g-C3N4 during thermal treatment.[, ] The formation of metallic Co after annealing was also confirmed by XRD and implied the successful formation of Co/N-CNT hybrids.

NiP nanosheet array electrode was prepared from the commercial Ni foam (NF) through a direct phosphorization strategy.[, ] Prior to phosphorization, the NF was sonicated for 30 min in 1.0 m HCl solution, acetone and ethanol, respectively. Next, 0.14 g NF (ca. 1 × 4 cm2) and 1.0 g red phosphorous powder were piled into the porcelain boat individually, where red phosphorous powder was closer to the upstream side. The precursors were reacted in Ar atmosphere for 1 h at 500 °C with a heating rate of 5 °C min−1. The NiP nanosheet array electrode was obtained after the tube furnace falling to room temperature.
